description: The Storage interface (storage.go) is clean but the Dolt implementation uses Dolt-specific features extensively without abstraction: DOLT_COMMIT, DOLT_PUSH/PULL/FETCH, dolt_diff(), dolt_history_*, dolt_branches, dolt_log, DOLT_HASHOF, DOLT_CONFLICTS_RESOLVE, AS OF syntax. These are used in versioned.go, federation.go, history.go, store.go. If a non-Dolt backend is ever needed, the VersionedStorage and FederatedStorage interfaces would need to be optional capabilities, not assumed.
title: Dolt-specific procedures not behind interface - blocks portability

description:
Code health review found several oversized files:

1. doctor.go - 2948 lines, 48 functions mixed together
   - Should split into doctor/checks/*.go for individual diagnostics
   - applyFixes() and previewFixes() are nearly identical

2. sync.go - 2121 lines
   - ZFC (Zero Flush Check) logic embedded inline (lines 213-247)
   - Multiple mode handlers should be extracted

3. init.go - 1732 lines
4. compact.go - 1097 lines
5. show.go - 1069 lines

Recommendation: Extract into focused sub-packages or split into logical files.
title: Split large cmd/bd files: doctor.go (2948 lines), sync.go (2121 lines)

close_reason: Merged to main (commit 0cb7936f). Circuit breaker implemented by obsidian, MR bd-wisp-hcao82 merged by refinery.
description: When Dolt is down, every agent independently retries for 30 seconds before failing. With N agents, that's N×30s of wasted time and connection storms. Implement a circuit breaker: after K consecutive failures within T seconds, trip the breaker and fail-fast for a cooldown period. Options: (1) file-based shared state in /tmp, (2) in-process with cenkalti/backoff already in deps, (3) lightweight lockfile protocol. The breaker should log clearly when tripped/reset so agents know why bd commands are failing fast.
notes: Implemented file-based circuit breaker for Dolt connections. State shared at /tmp/beads-dolt-circuit-<port>.json. Trips after 5 consecutive connection failures in 60s, 30s cooldown before half-open probe. Integrated into withRetry() and newServerMode(). Added isConnectionError() to distinguish server-down errors from query errors. OTel metrics: circuit_trips, circuit_rejected. 12 new unit tests, all passing.
title: P1: Circuit breaker for Dolt server connections

description:
bd doctor should detect old beads integration patterns and recommend migrating to bd prime approach.

## Current behavior
- bd doctor checks if Claude hooks are installed globally
- Doesn't check project-level integration (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md)
- Doesn't recommend migration for repos using old patterns

## Desired behavior
bd doctor should detect and suggest:

1. **Old slash command pattern detected**
   - Check for /beads:* references in AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md
   - Suggest: These slash commands are deprecated, use bd prime hooks instead
   
2. **No agent documentation**
   - Check if AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md exists
   - Suggest: Run 'bd onboard' or 'bd setup claude' to document workflow
   
3. **Old MCP-only pattern**
   - Check for instructions to use MCP tools but no bd prime hooks
   - Suggest: Add bd prime hooks for better token efficiency

4. **Migration path**
   - Show: 'Run bd setup claude to add SessionStart/PreCompact hooks'
   - Show: 'Update AGENTS.md to reference bd prime instead of slash commands'

## Example output

⚠ Warning: Old beads integration detected in CLAUDE.md
  Found: /beads:* slash command references (deprecated)
  Recommend: Migrate to bd prime hooks for better token efficiency
  Fix: Run 'bd setup claude' and update CLAUDE.md

💡 Tip: bd prime + hooks reduces token usage by 80-99% vs slash commands
  MCP mode: ~50 tokens vs ~10.5k for full MCP scan
  CLI mode: ~1-2k tokens with automatic context recovery

## Benefits
- Helps existing repos adopt new best practices
- Clear migration path for users
- Better token efficiency messaging
title: bd doctor should recommend bd prime migration for existing repos

description: wisps.go:1121-1141: addWispLabel and removeWispLabel perform INSERT/DELETE on wisp_labels but do NOT record events in wisp_events. In contrast, the permanent AddLabel/RemoveLabel (labels.go) DO record events via execContext INSERT INTO events. This means wisp label changes have no audit trail. Users running 'bd log' on a wisp won't see label changes. Suggested fix: add recordEventInTable calls for EventLabelAdded/EventLabelRemoved, matching labels.go behavior.
title: addWispLabel/removeWispLabel don't record events

description:
# Problem

The cmd/bd test suite is painfully slow:
- **279 tests** in cmd/bd alone
- Full suite takes **8+ minutes** to run
- Even with the 16 slowest integration tests now tagged with `integration` build tag, the remaining tests still take forever

This makes the development loop unusable. We can't wait 8+ minutes every time we want to run tests.

# Root Cause Analysis

## 1. Sheer Volume
279 tests is too many for a single package. Even at 0.1s per test, that's 28 seconds minimum just for cmd/bd.

## 2. Each Test Creates Full Database + Temp Directories
Every test does heavy setup:
- Creates temp directory (`t.TempDir()` or `os.MkdirTemp`)
- Initializes SQLite database
- Sets up git repo in many cases
- Creates full storage layer

Example from the tests:
```go
func setupCLITestDB(t *testing.T) string {
    tmpDir := createTempDirWithCleanup(t)
    runBDInProcess(t, tmpDir, "init", "--prefix", "test", "--quiet")
    return tmpDir
}
```

This happens 279 times!

## 3. Tests Are Not Properly Categorized
We have three types of tests mixed together:
- **Unit tests** - should be fast, test single functions
- **Integration tests** - test full workflows, need DB/git
- **End-to-end tests** - test entire CLI commands

They're all lumped together in cmd/bd, all running every time.

# What We've Already Fixed

Added `integration` build tags to 16 obviously-slow test files:
- import_profile_test.go (performance benchmarking tests)
- export_mtime_test.go (tests with time.Sleep calls)
- cli_fast_test.go (full CLI integration tests)
- delete_test.go, import_uncommitted_test.go, sync_local_only_test.go (git integration)
- And 10 more in internal/ packages

These are now excluded from the default `go test ./...` run.

# Proposed Solutions

## Option 1: Shared Test Fixtures (Quick Win)
Create a shared test database that multiple tests can use:
```go
var testDB *sqlite.SQLiteStorage
var testDBOnce sync.Once

func getSharedTestDB(t *testing.T) storage.Storage {
    testDBOnce.Do(func() {
        // Create one DB for all tests
    })
    return testDB
}
```

**Pros**: Easy to implement, immediate speedup
**Cons**: Tests become less isolated, harder to debug failures

## Option 2: Table-Driven Tests (Medium Win)
Collapse similar tests into table-driven tests:
```go
func TestCreate(t *testing.T) {
    tests := []struct{
        name string
        args []string
        want string
    }{
        {"basic issue", []string{"create", "Test"}, "created"},
        {"with description", []string{"create", "Test", "-d", "desc"}, "created"},
        // ... 50 more cases
    }
    
    db := setupOnce(t) // Setup once, not 50 times
    for _, tt := range tests {
        t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
            // test using shared db
        })
    }
}
```

**Pros**: Dramatically reduces setup overhead, tests run in parallel
**Cons**: Requires refactoring, tests share more state

## Option 3: Split cmd/bd Tests Into Packages (Big Win)
Move tests into focused packages:
- `cmd/bd/internal/clitests` - CLI integration tests (mark with integration tag)
- `cmd/bd/internal/unittests` - Fast unit tests
- Keep only essential tests in cmd/bd

**Pros**: Clean separation, easy to run just fast tests
**Cons**: Requires significant refactoring

## Option 4: Parallel Execution (Quick Win)
Add `t.Parallel()` to independent tests:
```go
func TestSomething(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel() // Run this test concurrently with others
    // ...
}
```

**Pros**: Easy to add, can cut time in half on multi-core machines
**Cons**: Doesn't reduce actual test work, just parallelizes it

## Option 5: In-Memory Databases (Medium Win)
Use `:memory:` SQLite databases instead of file-based:
```go
store, err := sqlite.New(ctx, ":memory:")
```

**Pros**: Faster than disk I/O, easier cleanup
**Cons**: Some tests need actual file-based DBs (export/import tests)

# Recommended Approach

**Short-term (this week)**:
1. Add `t.Parallel()` to all independent tests in cmd/bd
2. Use `:memory:` databases where possible
3. Create table-driven tests for similar test cases

**Medium-term (next sprint)**:
4. Split cmd/bd tests into focused packages
5. Mark more integration tests appropriately

**Long-term (backlog)**:
6. Consider shared test fixtures with proper isolation

# Current Status

We've tagged 16 files with `integration` build tag, but the remaining 279 tests in cmd/bd still take 8+ minutes. This issue tracks fixing the cmd/bd test performance specifically.

# Target

Get `go test ./...` (without `-short` or `-tags=integration`) down to **under 30 seconds**.


# THE REAL ROOT CAUSE (Updated Analysis)

After examining the actual test code, the problem is clear:

## Every Test Creates Its Own Database From Scratch

Look at `create_test.go`:
```go
func TestCreate_BasicIssue(t *testing.T) {
    tmpDir := t.TempDir()                          // ← Creates temp dir
    testDB := filepath.Join(tmpDir, ".beads", "beads.db")
    s := newTestStore(t, testDB)                   // ← Opens NEW SQLite connection
                                                    // ← Runs migrations
                                                    // ← Sets config
    // ... actual test (3 lines)
}

func TestCreate_WithDescription(t *testing.T) {
    tmpDir := t.TempDir()                          // ← Creates ANOTHER temp dir
    testDB := filepath.Join(tmpDir, ".beads", "beads.db")
    s := newTestStore(t, testDB)                   // ← Opens ANOTHER SQLite connection
    // ... actual test (3 lines)
}
```

**This happens 279 times!**

## These Tests Don't Need Isolation!

Most tests are just checking:
- "Can I create an issue with a title?"
- "Can I create an issue with a description?"
- "Can I add labels?"

They don't conflict with each other. They could all share ONE database!

## The Fix: Test Suites with Shared Setup

Instead of:
```go
func TestCreate_BasicIssue(t *testing.T) {
    s := newTestStore(t, t.TempDir()+"/db")  // ← Expensive!
    // test
}

func TestCreate_WithDesc(t *testing.T) {
    s := newTestStore(t, t.TempDir()+"/db")  // ← Expensive!
    // test
}
```

Do this:
```go
func TestCreate(t *testing.T) {
    // ONE setup for all subtests
    s := newTestStore(t, t.TempDir()+"/db")
    
    t.Run("basic_issue", func(t *testing.T) {
        t.Parallel()  // Can run concurrently - tests don't conflict
        // test using shared `s`
    })
    
    t.Run("with_description", func(t *testing.T) {
        t.Parallel()
        // test using shared `s`
    })
    
    // ... 50 more subtests, all using same DB
}
```

**Result**: 50 tests → 1 database setup instead of 50!

## Why This Works

SQLite is fine with concurrent reads and isolated transactions. These tests:
- ✅ Create different issues (no ID conflicts)
- ✅ Just read back what they created
- ✅ Don't depend on database state from other tests

They SHOULD share a database!

## Real Numbers

Current:
- 279 tests × (create dir + init SQLite + migrations) = **8 minutes**

After fix:
- 10 test suites × (create dir + init SQLite + migrations) = **30 seconds**
- 279 subtests running in parallel using those 10 DBs = **5 seconds**

**Total: ~35 seconds instead of 8 minutes!**

## Implementation Plan

1. **Group related tests** into suites (Create, List, Update, Delete, etc.)
2. **One setup per suite** instead of per test
3. **Use t.Run() for subtests** with t.Parallel()
4. **Keep tests that actually need isolation** separate (export/import tests, git operations)

This is way better than shuffling tests into folders!
notes: Analysis: The test panic in TestInitAlreadyInitialized is caused by store.Close() in PersistentPostRun setting s.db=nil, then subsequent HasRemote() call accessing the nil db. Also: suite is at ~27-37s when it actually completes (before panic kills it). Already well under 2min target. Main remaining work: fix the panic, consolidate metadata_filter_test.go (9 stores), metadata_ready_test.go (4 stores), list_test.go (7 stores), thread_test.go (5 stores).
title: cmd/bd test suite is absurdly slow - 279 tests taking 8+ minutes

description: schema.go ready_issues and blocked_issues views hardcode status IN (open, in_progress, blocked, deferred, hooked). Custom statuses configured via status.custom config key are validated but NOT included in these views. An issue with custom status 'review' would be permanently invisible to GetReadyWork and bd ready. Same pattern in dependencies.go:779, :834, :877 (IsBlocked, GetNewlyUnblockedByClose). ZFC violation: storage layer decides which statuses are 'active' without consulting config.
notes: Fixed all 8 hardcoded status IN clauses across schema.go (4), dependencies.go (3), queries.go (2). Changed from IN ('open','in_progress','blocked','deferred','hooked') to NOT IN ('closed','pinned'). Added 4 regression tests for custom status visibility in IsBlocked, GetNewlyUnblockedByClose, GetReadyWork, GetBlockedIssues. All tests pass.
title: SQL views hardcode status lists - custom statuses invisible to GetReadyWork

description:
Implement a bd status command that provides a quick snapshot of the issue database state, similar to how git status shows working tree state.

Expected output: Show summary including counts by state (open, in-progress, blocked, closed), recent activity (last 7 days), and quick overview without needing multiple queries.

Example output showing issue counts, recent activity stats, and pointer to bd list for details.

Proposed options: --all (show all issues), --assigned (show issues assigned to current user), --json (JSON format output)

Use cases: Quick project health check, onboarding for new contributors, integration with shell prompts or CI/CD, daily standup reference
title: Add 'bd status' command for issue database overview

description: dependencies.go:16-147: AddDependency and RemoveDependency use raw s.db.BeginTx + tx.Commit() but do NOT call DOLT_COMMIT afterwards. Compare with RunInTransaction (transaction.go:46-89) which explicitly calls DOLT_COMMIT('-Am', ...) after sqlTx.Commit(). This means dependency changes persist in the working set but are NOT recorded in Dolt's version history (dolt log, time-travel queries). They only become versioned when a later operation happens to call DOLT_COMMIT. Suggested fix: either call DOLT_COMMIT after tx.Commit() in these methods, or refactor to use RunInTransaction.
title: AddDependency/RemoveDependency bypass Dolt versioning

notes: Implemented: Added guard in create.go to reject positional titles starting with '-' as likely misinterpreted flags. Error message directs users to --title flag for intentional use. Integration test covers -- --help, -- --version, -- -p, and -- --foo-bar patterns, plus verifies --title still accepts dash-prefixed values.

description:
All beads read operations should validate database is in sync with JSONL before proceeding.

**Current Behavior:**
- Commands can query/read from stale database
- Only mutation operations (like 'bd sync') check if JSONL is newer
- User gets incorrect results without realizing database is out of sync

**Expected Behavior:**
- All beads commands should have pre-flight check for database freshness
- If JSONL is newer than database, refuse to operate with error: "Database out of sync. Run 'bd import' first."
- Same safety check that exists for 'bd sync' should apply to ALL operations

**Impact:**
- Users make decisions based on incomplete/outdated data
- Silent failures lead to confusion (e.g., thinking issues don't exist when they do)
- Similar to running git commands on stale repo without being warned to pull

**Example:**
- Searched for bd-g9eu issue file: not found
- Issue exists in .beads/issues.jsonl (in git)
- Database was stale, but no warning was given
- Led to incorrect conclusion that issue was already closed/deleted
notes: Implementation: Added staleness check in PersistentPreRun for read-only commands. Compares issues.jsonl mtime vs last_import_time metadata. Errors with actionable message when stale. Respects --allow-stale flag. Gracefully skips when no JSONL, no metadata, or corrupted metadata. New files: staleness.go, staleness_test.go. Modified: main.go (9 lines added).
title: Beads commands operate on stale database without warning

description:
The sync.git-remote config key is defined in yaml_config.go and BootstrapFromGitRemote() exists in bootstrap.go, but bd init never reads this config to auto-clone from a Dolt git remote.

When a user sets sync.git-remote in config.yaml (or ~/.config/bd/config.yaml) and runs bd init on a new machine, it should:
1. Detect sync.git-remote is configured
2. Call BootstrapFromGitRemote() to clone the Dolt database from the git remote
3. Skip fresh schema creation (database already has schema from clone)
4. Configure the remote in the new store so bd dolt push/pull work immediately

This enables the new-machine story for Dolt replication users: set the config once, bd init does the rest.
notes: Implemented: bd init now reads sync.git-remote from config.yaml and calls BootstrapFromGitRemoteWithDB() before dolt.New(). After store creation, adds the remote via store.AddRemote() so push/pull work immediately. All non-fatal with graceful fallback to fresh init.
title: Wire sync.git-remote config into bd init for auto-bootstrap

description: MEDIUM: hooks.go has three related issues: (1) uninstallHooks() (lines 542-598) never resets core.hooksPath after uninstall. If hooks were installed with --beads or --shared, core.hooksPath still points to .beads/hooks/ after uninstall, silently breaking all hooks. (2) --chain flag (lines 317-319) produces misleading success message claiming existing hooks renamed to .old -- this is a lie since section markers handle it differently. (3) Hook help text (lines 287-294) claims pre-commit syncs database, post-merge syncs, etc. -- none of this actually happens. Fix: reset hooksPath on uninstall, remove misleading --chain message, update help text.
title: Hook uninstall does not reset core.hooksPath + misleading --chain flag

description: Old bd versions (<0.24.0) installed merge driver with invalid placeholders %L %R instead of %A %B. description:
Gas Town and similar multi-agent systems need to configure separate beads databases per workspace/rig, distinct from any project-level beads.

## Use Case

In Gas Town:
- Each 'rig' (managed project) has multiple agents (polecats, refinery, witness)
- All agents in a rig should share a single beads database at the rig level
- This should be separate from any .beads/ the project itself uses
- The BEADS_DIR env var enables this

## Documentation Needed

1. Add a section to docs explaining BEADS_DIR for multi-agent setups
2. Example: setting BEADS_DIR in agent startup scripts/hooks
3. Clarify interaction with project-level .beads/ (BEADS_DIR takes precedence)

## Current Support

Already implemented in internal/beads/beads.go:FindDatabasePath():
- BEADS_DIR env var is checked first (preferred)
- BEADS_DB env var still supported (deprecated)
- Falls back to .beads/ search in tree

Just needs documentation for the multi-agent workspace pattern.
title: Document BEADS_DIR pattern for multi-agent workspaces (Gas Town)

description:
The beads MCP server (v0.23.1) successfully connects to Claude Code, but all tools fail to load with a schema validation error due to a bug in FastMCP 2.13.1.

Error: "Invalid literal value, expected \"object\"" in outputSchema.

Root Cause: FastMCP generates outputSchema with $ref at root level without "type": "object" for self-referential models (Issue).

Workaround: Use slash commands (/beads:ready) or wait for FastMCP fix.
title: Workaround for FastMCP outputSchema bug in Claude Code

If the walk fails partway through (permission error, filesystem error), a partial report is returned without any indication it was incomplete. Callers report 'No classic artifacts detected' when the scan actually failed.

2. config_values.go lines ~370-374: checkDatabaseConfigValues returns empty issues slice when dolt.NewFromConfigWithOptions fails. Database corruption or permission issues are masked as 'all config values valid'.

Suggested fix: Return or report errors instead of swallowing them.

description: DoltDockerImage constant, FindFreePort(), and WaitForServer() are identically defined in both testdoltserver.go (non-windows) and testdoltserver_windows.go (windows). These are platform-independent: DoltDockerImage is a string constant, FindFreePort is pure TCP, WaitForServer is pure TCP. Duplication means version pin changes or bug fixes must be applied to both files, risking drift. Fix: extract these into a shared file without build tags (e.g., testdoltcommon.go), keeping only the platform-divergent container logic in the build-tagged files.

description: wisps.go:384-385 and transaction.go:284-285: json.Marshal(value) for the waiters field discards the error with '_'. If marshaling fails (e.g., value contains an unsupported type like a channel), an empty or invalid JSON string is written to the database. This could corrupt the waiters field silently. Suggested fix: return the error: 'waitersJSON, err := json.Marshal(value); if err \!= nil { return fmt.Errorf("invalid waiters: %w", err) }'

description:
Tim Sehn confirmed (2026-02-28) that DOLT_RESET --soft + DOLT_COMMIT is safe on a running server. No downtime needed. The Compactor Dog (compactor_dog.go) should be updated to:

1. Run flatten via SQL instead of stopping the server
2. Use: SET @init = (SELECT commit_hash FROM dolt_log ORDER BY date ASC LIMIT 1); CALL DOLT_RESET('--soft', @init); CALL DOLT_COMMIT('-Am', 'daily compaction');
3. Remove all server stop/start logic from compaction path
4. Auto-GC is already on (since Dolt 1.75.0) so no manual gc needed

Concurrent writes during flatten are safe - merge base shifts but diff is just the txn.

Reference: dolt-storage.md (updated 2026-02-28)
title: Compactor Dog: use SQL-based flatten on running server

description:
When a Dolt remote is configured, auto-push after write operations (create, update, close, etc.) with a 5-minute debounce — similar to how JSONL auto-backup throttles at 15 minutes.

Implementation approach:
- Same pattern as maybeAutoBackup(): check last push timestamp, skip if < 5min ago
- Gate on store.HasRemote(ctx, 'origin') — only if a remote exists
- Run in PersistentPostRun after auto-commit (and after auto-backup)
- Failure is non-fatal (warning only, like JSONL git push)
- Configurable interval via config key (e.g. dolt.auto-push-interval: 5m)
- Opt-out via dolt.auto-push: false

This completes the Dolt replication story: users add a remote once, and data flows automatically.
notes: Implemented auto-push to Dolt remote with 5-minute debounce. New file: cmd/bd/dolt_autopush.go. Config keys: dolt.auto-push (auto-enabled when origin exists), dolt.auto-push-interval (default 5m). Same pattern as maybeAutoBackup: debounce, change detection, non-fatal warnings. Tests in dolt_autopush_test.go.
title: Auto-push to Dolt remote with 5-minute debounce

description: cmd/bd/doctor takes 44s. Doctor runs diagnostic checks — tests may be spawning real infrastructure. Check for: mocking opportunities, shared fixtures, parallel execution. Target: under 15s.
notes: Analysis: Tests take 68s. Root cause: 15+ tests each create a separate Dolt database (CREATE DATABASE + schema init = ~0.5-2s each). Fix: refactor tests to use branch-per-test isolation on the shared DB (newTestDoltStore). The path-based check functions (CheckDuplicateIssues, CheckStaleClosedIssues, etc.) internally open stores, so tests calling them create new DBs. Fix by testing the underlying query logic directly with the shared store's sql.DB.
title: Speed up cmd/bd/doctor tests (44s)

description: In cmd/bd/dolt.go doltRemoteAddCmd (line 646), the CLI remote add path calls doltutil.AddCLIRemote(dbPath, name, url) where name and url come directly from user args (args[0], args[1]). In doltutil/remotes.go:46, AddCLIRemote passes these as exec.Command arguments which is safe (Go's exec.Command does not invoke a shell). However, the Warning message on line 647 suggests running: 'cd %s && dolt remote add %s %s' which interpolates the name and url into a shell command suggestion. If a user copies that command, and name/url contain shell metacharacters, this could be exploited. The same pattern exists in RemoveCLIRemote's error message (dolt.go:821). Low severity since it's just a suggested command, but worth noting.
title: dolt remote add: command injection via unsanitized name/url in CLI remote fallback

Use cases: testing with different TTL values, per-repository TTL configuration, debugging with short TTL, supporting --ttl flag in bd merge command (mentioned in design doc bd-zvg). description:
When running 'bd import' on a fresh database (no existing issues), the command reports 'Import complete: 0 created, 0 updated' even though it successfully imported all issues from the JSONL file.

**Steps to reproduce:**
1. Delete .beads/beads.db
2. Run: bd import .beads/issues.jsonl
3. Observe output: 'Import complete: 0 created, 0 updated'
4. Run: bd list
5. Confirm: All issues are actually present in the database

**Expected behavior:**
Report the actual number of issues imported, e.g., 'Import complete: 523 created, 0 updated'

**Actual behavior:**
'Import complete: 0 created, 0 updated' (misleading - makes user think import failed)

**Impact:**
- Users think import failed when it succeeded
- Confusing during database sync operations (e.g., after git pull)
- Makes debugging harder (can't tell if import actually worked)

**Context:**
Discovered during VC session when syncing database after git pull. The misleading message caused confusion about whether the database was properly synced with the canonical JSONL file.
title: Import reports misleading '0 created, 0 updated' when actually importing all issues

description: In cmd/bd/dolt.go testHTTPConnectivity (line 1241), the code checks if host 'Contains ":"' to detect if a port is already present. But IPv6 addresses also contain colons (e.g., https://[::1]:8080/path). After stripping the scheme and path, an IPv6 address like '[::1]:8080' would match the Contains(':') check, setting port to '' and using the raw host as addr. The resulting dial would fail or connect to the wrong endpoint. Should use net.SplitHostPort or check for bracket-enclosed IPv6 first.
title: testHTTPConnectivity: incorrect parsing when host contains colon (IPv6)

description:
## Problem

The beads git merge driver is configured with invalid Git placeholders:

```
git config merge.beads.driver "bd merge %A %O %L %R"
```

Git doesn't recognize `%L` or `%R` as valid merge driver placeholders. The valid placeholders are:
- `%O` = base (common ancestor)
- `%A` = current version (ours)
- `%B` = other version (theirs)

## Impact

- Affects ALL users when they have `.beads/beads.jsonl` merge conflicts
- Automatic JSONL merge fails with error: "error reading left file: failed to open file: open 7: no such file or directory"
- Users must manually resolve conflicts instead of getting automatic merge

## Root Cause

The `bd init` command (or wherever the merge driver is configured) is using non-standard placeholders. When Git encounters `%L` and `%R`, it either passes them literally or interprets them incorrectly.

## Fix

Update the merge driver configuration to:
```
git config merge.beads.driver "bd merge %A %O %A %B"
```

Where:
- 1st `%A` = output file (current file, will be overwritten)
- `%O` = base (common ancestor)
- 2nd `%A` = left/current version
- `%B` = right/other version

## Action Items

1. Fix `bd init` (or equivalent setup command) to use correct placeholders
2. Add migration/warning for existing users with misconfigured merge driver
3. Update documentation with correct merge driver setup
4. Consider adding validation when `bd init` is run
notes: Implemented: Added FixMergeDriver() function and wired it into bd doctor --fix. The check already correctly detected %L/%R but the fix path was missing from applyFixList switch. Also added tests.
title: Git merge driver uses invalid placeholders (%L, %R instead of %A, %B)

description:
Two functions use N+1 query patterns that fire individual queries in a loop:

1. deep.go checkMoleculeIntegrity (~lines 482-496): runs a separate db.QueryRow for each molecule found (up to 100), issuing 101 queries. Could be done in a single JOIN query.

2. migration_validation.go compareDoltWithJSONL (~lines 429-443): calls store.GetIssue for each JSONL ID individually. For large migrations with thousands of issues, this fires thousands of queries. A single SELECT followed by set-difference in Go would be far more efficient.

Suggested fix: Replace per-item queries with batch queries or JOINs.
title: N+1 query patterns in deep.go and migration_validation.go

description: history.go:15: validRefPattern = ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$ rejects branch names containing dots or slashes. Dolt and git branch names commonly contain dots (e.g., 'release/v2.0', 'feature/auth.flow'). This pattern is used by getIssueAsOf, Diff, and CommitExists. Callers using branch names with dots or slashes will get 'invalid ref format' errors. Suggested fix: expand the pattern to allow dots and forward slashes: ^[a-zA-Z0-9_./-]+$ or use a more permissive pattern that excludes only dangerous characters (quotes, semicolons, backticks).
title: validateRef rejects valid Dolt branch names with dots or slashes

description:
Solo users get automatic JSONL backup to GitHub (auto-enabled when git remote exists), but there's no restore path. If a user loses their Dolt database (machine crash, new clone, etc.), they have .beads/backup/*.jsonl on GitHub but must manually bd init + bd import to recover.

Add a 'bd restore' command that:
1. Detects .beads/backup/*.jsonl files (or accepts a path)
2. Creates/reinitializes the Dolt database if needed
3. Imports all JSONL tables (issues, events, comments, dependencies, labels, config)
4. Restores the backup_state.json watermarks so incremental backup resumes correctly

Also consider: bd init should detect existing backup files and offer to restore automatically.

Without this, the auto-backup is a false sense of security — data is on GitHub but recovery is undocumented and manual.
title: Add 'bd restore' command to bootstrap from JSONL backup

description:
Tim Sehn suggested using Dolt's built-in MySQL scheduled events (server-maintained cron) for automatic compaction instead of our Dog system. This could simplify the architecture.

Reference: https://www.dolthub.com/blog/2023-10-02-scheduled-events/

Investigate:
1. Can we CREATE EVENT for daily flatten on each database?
2. Does it survive server restart?
3. Can it replace the Compactor Dog entirely?
4. Test with: CREATE EVENT daily_compact ON SCHEDULE EVERY 1 DAY DO BEGIN SET @init = (SELECT commit_hash FROM dolt_log ORDER BY date ASC LIMIT 1); CALL DOLT_RESET('--soft', @init); CALL DOLT_COMMIT('-Am', 'auto-compact'); END;

This is exploration/spike work - may or may not replace the Dog approach.
title: Explore Dolt scheduled events for automatic compaction

description:
Tried: bd list --state open
Error: unknown flag: --state
Expected: Filter issues by state (open, closed, deferred, in_progress, etc.)

Current workaround: bd list shows open by default, bd list --all shows everything. But there's no way to filter to just closed, just deferred, etc. without post-processing.

Every major CLI issue tracker supports --state filtering. This is a natural expectation for any agent or user.

Suggested: Add --state flag accepting open|closed|deferred|in_progress (or a comma-separated list). Could alias --status as well.

title: Add --state flag to bd list as filter (desire path)

description: In credentials.go setFederationCredentials (line 264), DOLT_REMOTE_USER and DOLT_REMOTE_PASSWORD are set as process-wide environment variables. The federationEnvMutex serializes federation operations, but it does NOT protect against non-federation code that might also read these env vars or against other goroutines that might exec dolt CLI commands. In store.go Push/Pull, the SQL CALL is made between Setenv and Unsetenv — if another goroutine runs a dolt CLI command (like doltCLIPush) during that window, it would inherit the federation credentials. The env-var approach is fundamentally fragile for concurrent use.
notes: Analysis: The race is between CLI subprocess env inheritance and process-wide Setenv. Fix: pass credentials to CLI subprocesses via cmd.Env instead of os.Setenv. SQL path must keep mutex+env (unavoidable for in-process Dolt SQL server).
title: setFederationCredentials: race window between env set and SQL exec

description: The merge package uses string for status comparison (e.g., result.Status == closed, issue.Status == StatusTombstone). The types package defines Status as a type alias with validation. While the merge package needs its own Issue struct for JSONL flexibility, it could import and use types.Status for constants to get compile-time type checking. Current code: if left == closed || right == closed. Could be: if left == string(types.StatusClosed). This is low priority since string comparison works correctly. Files: internal/merge/merge.go:44, 488, 501-521
title: Consider using types.Status in merge package for type safety

description: Review commit 725f6bbb in beads repo. Check: flag plumbing correctness, wisp creation with root_only=true, no children created, backward compatibility.
design:
## Code Review: --root-only flag in bd mol wisp create (725f6bbb)

**Verdict: LGTM — clean commit, no blocking issues.**

### 1. Flag Plumbing Correctness ✅

- `--root-only` registered on both `wispCmd` and `wispCreateCmd` (backward compat for both CLI paths)
- Read via `cmd.Flags().GetBool("root-only")` at wisp.go
- Passed through `CloneOptions{RootOnly: rootOnly}` → `spawnMoleculeWithOptions` → `cloneSubgraph`
- Full pipeline verified end-to-end

### 2. Wisp Creation with root_only=true ✅

- `cloneSubgraph` narrows `issuesToCreate = []*types.Issue{subgraph.Root}` when RootOnly is set
- Root issue goes through the identical creation path (assignee override, variable substitution, ephemeral marking, bonded ID generation)
- `idMapping` correctly contains exactly one entry (root only)

### 3. No Children Created ✅

- Children are excluded from `issuesToCreate` (only root is in the slice)
- Dependencies are skipped via `if opts.RootOnly { break }` in the second pass
- AttachToID still works (uses `idMapping[subgraph.Root.ID]` which is always populated)
- Dynamic bonding (`ParentID`) also works — root gets bonded ID as expected

### 4. Backward Compatibility ✅

- `spawnMolecule()` function is preserved — all existing callers (pour.go:216, 9 test call sites in mol_test.go) are unaffected
- Default `RootOnly` is `false` (Go zero value), so existing `CloneOptions{}` usage safe
- Dry-run path properly branches: root-only shows single-line summary with inlined step count; non-root-only path is unchanged

### 5. `Created` Field Fix ✅

- Changed from `len(subgraph.Issues)` to `len(idMapping)` — more accurate in ALL cases
- For non-root-only: `len(idMapping)` == `len(subgraph.Issues)` in all success paths (errors cause tx rollback)
- For root-only: correctly returns 1 instead of the full template count
- This is a semantic improvement, not just a root-only fix

### Minor Nit (Non-Blocking)

The dependency loop guard:
```go
for _, dep := range subgraph.Dependencies {
    if opts.RootOnly { break }
    ...
}
```
Would be slightly cleaner as:
```go
if !opts.RootOnly {
    for _, dep := range subgraph.Dependencies { ... }
}
```
Functionally identical — the `break` fires on first iteration and exits. But wrapping the loop is more idiomatic Go. Not worth a re-spin.

### Summary

Two files changed (+39/-9), well-scoped. The ~15x Dolt write reduction claim is plausible (typical patrol formula has 10+ steps). All edge cases (bonding, attachment, dry-run, backward compat) are covered.
title: Code review: --root-only flag in bd mol wisp create (725f6bbb)

description:
Several check functions in validation.go return StatusOK with message 'N/A (query failed)' when a database query fails. This masks real problems — a broken database connection produces an all-green doctor report.

Affected locations:
- checkOrphanedDependenciesDB (~line 163): query error returns Status: 'ok'
- CheckTestPollution (~line 314): same pattern
- checkDuplicateIssuesDB (~line 241): same pattern

Suggested fix: Return StatusWarning (not StatusOK) when a query fails, so users can distinguish 'check passed' from 'check could not run'.
title: Query errors masked as StatusOK in validation.go

description:
Multiple places parse config.yaml with custom structs:

1. **autoimport.go:148** - `localConfig{SyncBranch}`
2. **main.go:310** - strings.Contains for no-db (fragile, see bd-r6k2)
3. **doctor.go:863** - strings.Contains for no-db (fragile, see bd-r6k2)
4. **internal/config/config.go** - Uses viper (but caches at startup, problematic for tests)

Consider creating a shared utility in `internal/configfile/` or extending the viper config:

```go
// internal/configfile/yaml.go
type YAMLConfig struct {
    SyncBranch string `yaml:"sync-branch"`
    NoDb       bool   `yaml:"no-db"`
    IssuePrefix string `yaml:"issue-prefix"`
    Author     string `yaml:"author"`
}

func LoadYAML(beadsDir string) (*YAMLConfig, error) {
    // Parse config.yaml with proper YAML library
}
```

Benefits:
- Single source of truth for config.yaml structure
- Proper YAML parsing everywhere
- Easier to add new config fields

Trade-off: May add complexity for simple one-off reads.
title: Consider consolidating config.yaml parsing into shared utility

description:
Comprehensive improvements to bd command set based on 2025 audit findings.

## Background
See docs/command-audit-2025.md for detailed analysis.

## Goals
1. Standardize flag naming and behavior across all commands
2. Add missing flags for feature parity
3. Fix naming confusion
4. Improve consistency in JSON output

## Success Criteria
- All mutating commands support --dry-run (no --preview variants)
- bd update supports label operations
- bd search has filter parity with bd list
- Priority flags accept both int and P0-P4 format everywhere
- JSON output is consistent across all commands
title: Command Set Standardization & Flag Consistency

description:
Add consistent verbosity controls across all commands.

**Current state:**
- bd init has --quiet flag
- No other commands have verbosity controls
- Debug output controlled by BD_VERBOSE env var

**Proposal:**
Add persistent flags:
- --verbose / -v: Enable debug output
- --quiet / -q: Suppress non-essential output

**Implementation:**
- Add to rootCmd.PersistentFlags()
- Replace BD_VERBOSE checks with flag checks
- Standardize output levels:
  * Quiet: Errors only
  * Normal: Errors + success messages
  * Verbose: Errors + success + debug info

**Files to modify:**
- cmd/bd/main.go (add flags)
- internal/debug/debug.go (respect flags)
- Update all commands to respect quiet mode

**Testing:**
- Verify --verbose shows debug output
- Verify --quiet suppresses normal output
- Ensure errors always show regardless of mode
title: Add global verbosity flags (--verbose, --quiet)

description:

description:

**Currently only has:**

description:

**Commands to audit:**

description:
Clarify the difference between bd clean and bd cleanup to reduce user confusion.

**Current state:**
- bd clean: Remove temporary artifacts (.beads/bd.sock, logs, etc.)
- bd cleanup: Delete old closed issues from database

**Options:**
1. Rename for clarity:
   - bd clean → bd clean-temp
   - bd cleanup → bd cleanup-issues
   
2. Keep names but improve help text and documentation

3. Add prominent warnings in help output

**Preferred approach:** Option 2 (improve documentation)
- Update short/long descriptions in commands
- Add examples to help text
- Update README.md
- Add cross-references in help output

**Files to modify:**
- cmd/bd/clean.go
- cmd/bd/cleanup.go
- README.md or ADVANCED.md
title: Improve clean vs cleanup command naming/documentation

description:
Document use cases or consider deprecation for infrequently-used commands.

**Commands to review:**
1. bd rename-prefix - How often is this used? Document use cases
2. bd detect-pollution - Consider integrating into bd validate
3. bd migrate-hash-ids - One-time migration, keep but document as legacy

**For each command:**
- Document typical use cases
- Add examples to help text
- Consider if it should be a subcommand instead
- Add deprecation warning if appropriate

**Not changing:**
- duplicates ✓ (useful for data quality)
- repair-deps ✓ (useful for fixing broken refs)
- restore ✓ (critical for compacted issues)
- compact ✓ (performance feature)

**Deliverable:**
- Updated help text
- Documentation in ADVANCED.md
- Deprecation plan if needed
title: Review and document rarely-used commands

description:
80+ instances of inconsistent error handling across sqlite.go with mix of %w, %v, and no wrapping.

Location: internal/storage/sqlite/sqlite.go (throughout)

Problem:
- Some use fmt.Errorf("op failed: %w", err) - correct wrapping
- Some use fmt.Errorf("op failed: %v", err) - loses error chain
- Some return err directly - no context
- Hard to debug production issues
- Can't distinguish error types

Solution: Create internal/storage/sqlite/errors.go:
- Define sentinel errors (ErrNotFound, ErrInvalidID, etc.)
- Create wrapDBError(op string, err error) helper
- Convert sql.ErrNoRows to ErrNotFound
- Always wrap with operation context

Impact: Lost error context; inconsistent messages; hard to debug

Effort: 5-7 hours
notes:
Implemented centralized error handling in dolt storage layer:

1. Created internal/storage/dolt/errors.go with:
   - Sentinel errors: ErrTransaction, ErrQuery, ErrScan, ErrExec
   - Helper functions: wrapDBError, wrapTransactionError, wrapScanError, wrapQueryError, wrapExecError
   - wrapDBError converts sql.ErrNoRows → storage.ErrNotFound

2. Applied wrapping to ~85 bare return err patterns across 12 files:
   - transaction.go, queries.go, dependencies.go, issues.go, wisps.go
   - ephemeral_routing.go, credentials.go, versioned.go, labels.go
   - federation.go

3. Key design decisions:
   - scanIssueFrom left unwrapped: callers check sql.ErrNoRows explicitly
   - Final rows.Err() returns left idiomatic (caller provides context)
   - Store-level pass-throughs (withRetry, Close) left unwrapped
   - All existing fmt.Errorf wrapping preserved

4. Tests: All storage tests pass. Unit tests for error helpers included.
title: Centralize error handling patterns in storage layer

description:
In perf_dolt.go line ~108, runDoltServerDiagnostics constructs DSN as 'root:@tcp(...)' ignoring the configured user and BEADS_DOLT_PASSWORD environment variable. Meanwhile, openDoltDB in dolt.go correctly reads user/password from config and environment. If the server has a non-root user or requires a password, the perf diagnostics will fail or connect with wrong credentials.

Additionally, no context timeout is set on diagnostic queries (line ~122 uses bare context.Background()). Queries could hang indefinitely against a slow or deadlocked server. Other functions use context.WithTimeout consistently.

Suggested fix: Use the same credential resolution as openDoltDB. Add context.WithTimeout.
title: Hardcoded root: credentials in perf_dolt.go bypass configured authentication

The similarly-named printSuccess/printWarning (also in migrate_dolt.go) ARE used. The migrate* variants and printNoop appear to be leftover from a refactor.

Fix: Remove all three unused functions.
title: Dead functions: printNoop, migratePrintSuccess, migratePrintWarning

description: Review internal/storage/ (especially dolt/store.go) and internal/configfile/ for: hardcoded database names, hardcoded table structures that should be schema-driven, Go code making decisions that should be agent decisions, string matching on wisp content, hardcoded status transitions. Also check for Dolt-specific assumptions that would break portability. File beads for each issue found.
notes:
## Code Review Findings: Storage Layer

### Summary
Reviewed internal/storage/ (~14 files, ~5000 LOC) and internal/configfile/ (~1 file, 350 LOC).
Found 7 categories of issues with hardcoded assumptions and ZFC violations.

### 1. HARDCODED DATABASE NAME "beads" (4 locations)
- store.go:445 - applyConfigDefaults defaults to "beads" when no config
- bootstrap.go:43 - BootstrapFromGitRemoteWithDB defaults database to "beads"
- bootstrap.go:100 - schemaReady defaults dbName to "beads"
- configfile.go:47 - DefaultConfig uses "beads.db" for Database field

These should be centralized into a single constant or config resolution.

### 2. HARDCODED INFRA TYPES - Go code making agent-level decisions (2 locations)
- ephemeral_routing.go:22-27 - IsInfraType hardcodes "agent", "rig", "role", "message"
- migrations/007_infra_to_wisps.go:11 - infraTypes duplicates the same list

The storage layer DECIDES which types are "infrastructure" and routes them
to the wisps table. This is a policy decision that should be agent-configurable,
not hardcoded in Go. If Gas Town adds a new infra type (e.g., "formula", "slot"),
a code change + release is required.

### 3. HARDCODED STATUS LISTS IN SQL VIEWS (schema.go)
- schema.go:279 - ready_issues view: IN (open, in_progress, blocked, deferred, hooked)
- schema.go:326 - blocked_issues view: IN (open, in_progress, blocked, deferred, hooked)
- dependencies.go:779 - IsBlocked: IN (open, in_progress, blocked, deferred, hooked)
- dependencies.go:834 - GetNewlyUnblockedByClose: IN (open, blocked)
- dependencies.go:877 - GetNewlyUnblockedByClose: IN (open, in_progress, blocked, deferred, hooked)

Custom statuses (config key "status.custom") are supported for validation but
NOT reflected in these SQL views. A custom status like "review" would be
invisible to the ready_issues view, making issues with that status permanently
invisible to GetReadyWork.

### 4. HARDCODED STATUS IN COMPACT.GO - Go code deciding agent logic
- compact.go:35 - status != "closed" check uses string literal instead of types.StatusClosed
- compact.go:49,59 - Magic numbers for compaction days (30, 90) hardcoded, though
  config keys exist (compact_tier1_days, compact_tier2_days) they are not read.

### 5. STRING-MATCHING ON WISP CONTENT - ephemeral_routing.go
- ephemeral_routing.go:15 - IsEphemeralID checks strings.Contains(id, "-wisp-")

This is a convention-based routing that breaks when wisps have explicit IDs
(GH#2053 noted in code). The code already has workarounds (isActiveWisp,
partitionByWispStatus) but the fundamental pattern of routing by ID substring
is fragile.

### 6. DOLT-SPECIFIC ASSUMPTIONS THAT BREAK PORTABILITY
- store.go:848 - CALL DOLT_COMMIT during schema init
- versioned.go:52-62 - dolt_diff(), AS OF, DOLT_HASHOF() - Dolt-only functions
- versioned.go:142 - dolt_branches table query
- federation.go:19 - DOLT_PUSH, DOLT_PULL, DOLT_FETCH - Dolt procedures
- versioned.go:253 - DOLT_CONFLICTS_RESOLVE procedure
- store.go:795-796 - CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS with Dolt catalog race workarounds
- history.go:83-86 - dolt_history_issues table (Dolt-specific virtual table)
- versioned.go:204-212 - dolt_log table query

The Storage interface (storage.go) is clean, but the concrete Dolt implementation
uses Dolt-specific stored procedures and virtual tables extensively. If a
non-Dolt backend were added, the versioned/federation features would need
separate interface extension points.

### 7. HARDCODED DEFAULT CONFIG VALUES (schema.go:250-263)
- defaultConfig embeds specific defaults: compact_model = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
  compact_batch_size = 50, types.custom = specific list including "molecule,gate,convoy..."
- These are Gas Town operational constants baked into the storage layer schema init.

### Non-issues (well-designed patterns)
- Custom types/statuses: config.go reads from DB config table with YAML fallback - good
- issue_scan.go: Centralized scan via issueSelectColumns - avoids column drift
- configfile.go: All server params have env var overrides - good for portability
- transactions.go: Clean interface with proper commit semantics
- Test database firewall: Pattern-based guard against test pollution - solid defense
title: Code review: storage layer for hardcoded assumptions and ZFC violations

description: internal/storage/dolt/history_test.go:54-57 TestGetIssueHistory fails with 'result max1Row iterator returned more than one row'. The getIssueHistory() query uses max1Row but dolt_history_* tables return multiple versions per issue. Fix by paginating or aggregating history results with ORDER BY timestamp, or by removing the max1Row constraint and collecting all rows.
title: Fix history_test.go: max1Row iterator returns multiple rows from dolt_history

description:
In hooks_migration.go lines ~189-201, detectHookMarkerState uses strings.Contains to check for hookMarkerBeginTag and hookMarkerEndTag. If a hook file has been corrupted to contain two BEGIN tags and one END tag (or two full marker blocks), the function returns hookMarkerStateValid. It should verify exactly one BEGIN and one END exist, and that BEGIN precedes END, to correctly detect corruption.

Suggested fix: Count occurrences of each tag and verify order.
title: detectHookMarkerState does not validate marker count or order

description: cmd/bd/migrate_hooks_test.go lines 55 and 80 call formatHookMigrationPlan(plan, bool) but the function signature in migrate_hooks.go:78 only takes 1 parameter (plan). description: HIGH: mol_current.go AdvanceToNextStep() (lines 514-577) reads molecule progress, identifies next ready step, then writes to it -- all without a transaction. In multi-agent Gas Town, two agents could simultaneously close different steps, both identify the same next ready step, and both try to claim it. Classic read-then-write TOCTOU race. Fix: wrap read-identify-update in transact() or add optimistic concurrency control.
title: AdvanceToNextStep TOCTOU race condition in multi-agent environment

description: In cmd/bd/dolt.go doltRemoteAddCmd (lines 630-649), the remote is first added to the SQL server, then to CLI. If the SQL add succeeds but CLI fails, the code prints a warning but reports success ('Added remote ... (SQL + CLI)' on line 658). The user sees a success message with the '(SQL + CLI)' suffix even though CLI failed. The success message should accurately reflect which surfaces succeeded. Similarly in doltRemoteRemoveCmd (lines 807-838), if SQL removal succeeds but CLI removal fails, the success message says 'Removed remote ... (SQL + CLI)' which is misleading.

description:
HIGH: create.go createInRig() (lines 834-993) has two critical issues:

1. Missing Dolt commit for labels (lines 974-979): Labels added during cross-rig creation
   are not committed to Dolt. The main create path (lines 743-748) explicitly calls
   store.Commit() for post-create metadata, but createInRig never commits. Labels sit
   in the Dolt working set and can be lost on push/sync/restart. Same class of bug as GH#2009.

2. Major feature divergence: createInRig is missing compared to the main create path:
   - --deps flag (dependency creation)
   - --parent flag (parent-child relationships, label inheritance)
   - --estimate, --validate flags
   - --waits-for / --waits-for-gate flags
   - --no-inherit-labels handling
   - Agent label auto-add logic
   - Hook execution (hookRunner.Run)
   - SetLastTouchedID tracking
   - discoveredFromParentID / SourceRepo inheritance
   - MolType/WispType validation (no IsValid() check)
   - Event flag validation (event metadata on non-event types)

   Users creating cross-rig issues with any of these flags silently have them ignored.

Fix: Consolidate into a single code path operating on targetStore, or add all missing
features to createInRig. Consolidation strongly preferred to prevent ongoing divergence.
title: createInRig missing Dolt commit for labels and major feature divergence

description: Several tombstone merge tests use time.Now() to create test data: time.Now().Add(-24 * time.Hour), time.Now().Add(-60 * 24 * time.Hour), etc. While these work reliably in practice (24h vs 30d TTL has large margin), they could theoretically be flaky if: 1) Tests run slowly, 2) System clock changes during test, 3) TTL constants change. Recommendation: Consider using a fixed reference time or time injection for deterministic tests. Lower priority since current margin is large. Files: internal/merge/merge_test.go:1337-1338, 1352-1353, 1548-1549, 1590-1591
title: Time-dependent tests may be flaky near TTL boundary

Add subcommands:

- bd dolt remote list               — calls store.ListRemotes()  
- bd dolt remote remove <name>      — calls store.RemoveRemote()

CheckMigrationCompletion at line ~239 uses this to set result.DoltLocked, so a connection failure incorrectly reports DoltLocked: false.

description: events.go:61-78: GetAllEventsSince uses UNION ALL between events and wisp_events tables, both of which use independent auto-increment IDs. The result is ORDER BY id ASC, but both tables can have events with the same ID (e.g., both tables have row ID=5). This produces ambiguous ordering and can cause event-streaming consumers to miss events or process them out of order. Suggested fix: either add a table discriminator to the ORDER BY (e.g., ORDER BY table_source, id ASC), use UNION ALL with created_at ordering instead, or use a single shared sequence.
title: GetAllEventsSince UNION ALL has auto-increment ID collisions

design:
# Code Review: Storage Layer & Query Logic (internal/storage/dolt/*.go)

## Scope
26 non-test Go source files in internal/storage/dolt/ (excluding migrations/ subdirectory).

## Files Reviewed
store.go (~1100 LoC), transaction.go (580), queries.go (~1000+), ephemeral_routing.go (282), issues.go (~450), wisps.go (1142), events.go (320), labels.go (221), dependencies.go (1075), config.go (257), errors.go (72), util.go (39), issue_scan.go (199), open.go (141), circuit.go (240), compact.go (165), history.go (271), federation.go (244), rename.go (147), versioned.go (214), noms_lock.go (49), bootstrap.go (167), credentials.go (313), adaptive_length.go (130), metadata_schema.go (132), migrations.go (80)

## Issues Filed (14 beads)

### Security (1)
- bd-rv8g: Encryption key derivation from dbPath is predictable (credentials.go)

### Data Integrity (5)
- bd-2avi: AddDependency/RemoveDependency bypass Dolt versioning (dependencies.go)
- bd-hl48: GetAllEventsSince UNION ALL has auto-increment ID collisions (events.go)
- bd-xe27: Cycle detection does not span wisp_dependencies table (dependencies.go)
- bd-od40: UpdateIssueID does not update wisp table references (rename.go)
- bd-zzji: SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS=0 not restored on tx rollback (rename.go)

### Error Handling (3)
- bd-w59l: SearchIssues ephemeral fallthrough silently swallows errors (queries.go)
- bd-57ab: waiters JSON marshal error silently discarded (wisps.go, transaction.go)
- bd-n01d: PromoteFromEphemeral discards event/comment copy errors (ephemeral_routing.go)

### Concurrency (1)
- bd-klgl: getWisp RLock not deferred - panic causes permanent deadlock (wisps.go)

### API Inconsistency (1)
- bd-v6v8: Transaction SearchIssues supports far fewer filters than store-level (transaction.go)

### Maintenance / Code Quality (2)
- bd-is9q: Massive filter-building duplication between searchWisps and SearchIssues (wisps.go, queries.go)
- bd-0jph: addWispLabel/removeWispLabel do not record events (wisps.go)

### Correctness (1)
- bd-8ifp: validateRef rejects valid Dolt branch names with dots or slashes (history.go)

## Themes Observed

1. Wisp/permanent table parity gap: Many operations handle the permanent tables comprehensively but miss the wisp counterparts (rename, cycle detection, event recording, label event audit trail).

2. Inconsistent Dolt versioning: Some write paths use RunInTransaction (which calls DOLT_COMMIT), others use raw tx.Commit() without DOLT_COMMIT. This creates gaps in the version history.

3. Error handling philosophy inconsistency: Some paths use best-effort (_, _ = ...) while others are strict. The fallthrough pattern in SearchIssues silently masks errors under the guise of backwards compatibility.

4. Filter duplication: The two-table architecture (issues + wisps) requires parallel filter-building code that is not shared, creating ~400 lines of duplicated logic that must be kept in sync.

## No Issues Found In
- SQL injection: All user-supplied values use parameterized queries. Dynamic table names are hardcoded constants. Refs are validated with regex before string interpolation.
- Dead SQLite/JSONL references: None found. The codebase is clean of legacy storage references.
- OTel instrumentation: Properly applied to queryContext/execContext.
- Circuit breaker: Well-implemented with file-based cross-process state.
- Batch query patterns: Properly bounded with queryBatchSize to avoid oversized IN-clauses.
notes: Review complete. Read all 26 non-test Go source files in internal/storage/dolt/. Filed 14 issue beads covering security (1), data integrity (5), error handling (3), concurrency (1), API inconsistency (1), maintenance (2), and correctness (1). Key themes: wisp/permanent table parity gaps, inconsistent Dolt versioning, error swallowing patterns, filter code duplication. No SQL injection, dead SQLite/JSONL references, or OTel gaps found. Full findings in design field.
title: Review: Storage Layer & Query Logic

design:
## Doctor Subsystem Review — Findings Summary

Reviewed ~14,300 lines across 50 source files in cmd/bd/doctor/ and cmd/bd/doctor/fix/.

### Issues Filed (22 beads)

**High Severity (4):**
- bd-nk9f: Systematic missing rows.Err() checks (~14+ locations)
- bd-a97g: Query errors masked as StatusOK in validation.go
- bd-qa7l: CheckIDFormat always returns 'hash-based' regardless of detection
- bd-k8pu: ParseVersionParts index-out-of-bounds panic in version.go

**Medium Severity (14):**
- bd-l75n: Potential panic on short repo IDs [:8] in CheckRepoFingerprint
- bd-7zej: N+1 query patterns in deep.go and migration_validation.go
- bd-c1g8: Hardcoded root: credentials in perf_dolt.go
- bd-hwmb: os.Exit from library code in perf.go
- bd-lars: Nocgo stubs return StatusOK for skipped checks
- bd-md2p: Relative path usage fragile from subdirectories
- bd-7fsv: Hand-rolled YAML parser in multirepo.go is incorrect
- bd-hwxg: remotes.go Fix field bug
- bd-uz1x: Federation hardcoded port + wrong PID path
- bd-ppnp: fix/database_integrity.go backup restore bug
- bd-s20p: fix/validation.go openDoltDB missing timeout/password
- bd-bbr6: fix/maintenance.go StaleClosedIssues skip logic
- bd-d96s: detectHookMarkerState marker validation
- bd-6gs3: Inconsistent return shape from RunDoltHealthChecksWithLock

**Cleanup/Technical Debt (4):**

2. Error masking as StatusOK is widespread in validation checks

4. JSONL/SQLite references persist across ~10 files post-Dolt migration
5. Multiple check functions use relative paths instead of accepting root path parameter
6. The fix/ package has divergent DB-open functions (openDoltDB vs openFixDB)

# Review: Features & Integration — Full Findings

## Scope Reviewed
- cmd/bd/create.go (39KB)
- cmd/bd/close.go (12KB)
- cmd/bd/mol*.go (13 files)
- cmd/bd/hooks.go (30KB)
- cmd/bd/dep.go (34KB) + dep_test.go (40KB)
- cmd/bd/list.go (33KB)

## HIGH Severity (5 issues)

1. **bd-ey4f**: createInRig missing Dolt commit for labels AND major feature divergence
   - Labels not committed on cross-rig create (data loss)
   - Missing 10+ features vs main create path (silently ignored)

2. **bd-rev6**: ensureBeadsDirForPath checks for beads.db (stale SQLite artifact)
   - Existence check always fails for Dolt dirs, risking re-init

3. **bd-6py7**: burnWisps deletes without transaction
   - Partial deletion on failure, silently reports success

4. **bd-e1ev**: AdvanceToNextStep TOCTOU race condition
   - Two agents can claim same next step simultaneously

5. **bd-87xv**: watchIssues broken with Dolt
   - Watches for JSONL/.db file changes that never happen with Dolt
   - Signal handler leak and debounce timer race condition

## MEDIUM Severity (9 issues)

6. **bd-31bo**: Hook uninstall gaps (core.hooksPath not reset, misleading --chain, stale help)
7. **bd-gp5b**: Embedded hook templates unused (binary bloat, version drift v0.56.1)
8. **bd-agx1**: close.go swallowed GetIssue error + cross-rig autoClose assumption
9. **bd-jmzc**: dep.go: cannot remove external deps + dead --type flag + no type validation
10. **bd-ttno**: list.go missing input validation (--status, --sort, --type flags)
11. **bd-cg90**: Stale JSONL/SQLite references across codebase (5+ locations)
12. **bd-pr9v**: mol_stale.go --json flag shadows persistent root flag
13. **bd-kzp3**: findGateReadyMolecules hardcoded limits silently truncate
14. **bd-hz8h**: Missing test coverage for critical paths
15. **bd-hn4q**: findParentMolecule N+1 queries

## NOT Filed (LOW / cosmetic)
- Dead isRebaseInProgress() function (hooks.go)
- Wasteful strings.Builder in getHookVersion
- getPinnedMolecule shells out on every commit
- No msgFile path validation (defense-in-depth)
- Asymmetric blank line handling in inject/remove section
- close.go escalated flag produces identical behavior
- close.go SetLastTouchedID not called after close
- sanitizeFormulaName compiles regexes per call
- idToStepID can produce duplicate step IDs
- getVarNames non-deterministic order
- mol_ready_gated_test.go stale test setup
- TODOs in mol_squash.go and mol_bond.go
- minPriority semantics (0 vs unset)
- withStorage/dbPath dead code in list.go
- dep direction validation inconsistency
- rootBlocked ignores StatusBlocked children
- warnIfCyclesExist redundant after AddDependency
notes: Review complete. Found ~30 issues across scope files. Filing beads for 14 substantive groups (HIGH and MEDIUM). Skipping LOW/cosmetic. Main themes: (1) createInRig code path divergence with missing features/validation/commits, (2) stale SQLite/JSONL references from pre-Dolt era, (3) race conditions in molecule lifecycle, (4) broken watch mode, (5) missing input validation across multiple commands, (6) hook system install/uninstall gaps.

description: In internal/testutil/testdoltserver.go:195, StartIsolatedDoltContainer uses context.Background() without a timeout when calling dolt.Run(). The singleton startDoltContainer (line 115) correctly uses context.WithTimeout(ctx, serverStartTimeout). A slow Docker pull or unresponsive container start could hang tests indefinitely in StartIsolatedDoltContainer. Fix: add context.WithTimeout using serverStartTimeout, matching the singleton pattern.
title: Missing timeout in StartIsolatedDoltContainer

description: wisps.go:522-767 vs queries.go:16-~300: searchWisps() and SearchIssues() duplicate ~200 lines of filter-building logic for WHERE clauses. Both build identical filters (Status, ExcludeStatus, IssueType, Assignee, Priority, IDs, Labels, date ranges, etc.) but targeting different tables. Any new filter field must be manually added to both functions, creating a maintenance burden and risk of divergence. Multiple filters already differ subtly (e.g., SearchIssues handles filter.Assignee with OR logic for unassigned, searchWisps uses simple equality). Suggested fix: extract shared filter-building into a function like buildIssueFilterClauses(filter, tableName) that returns (whereClauses, args).
title: Massive filter-building duplication between searchWisps and SearchIssues

description: Fix N+1 queries, hardcoded limits, search filter duplication, missing test coverage, build-tag duplication, container timeouts, migration gating, and unused embedded templates.
title: Fix: Query Performance, Tests & Build

description: MEDIUM: dep.go has three related issues: (1) depRemoveCmd (lines 443-479) calls ResolvePartialID on both args, which fails for external: prefixed IDs. Unlike depAddCmd which has external: handling (lines 234-261), depRemoveCmd has none -- impossible to remove external deps via CLI. (2) depTreeCmd registers --type flag (line 1113) but Run function never reads it -- flag is silently ignored. (3) depAddCmd (line 274) casts depType to types.DependencyType without validation -- garbage stored in DB. Fix: add external ref handling to remove, implement or remove --type flag, add type validation.
title: dep.go: cannot remove external deps + dead --type flag on depTree

close_reason: Fixed: added length check before indexing ParseVersionParts result
description:
In version.go lines ~156-161, CheckMetadataVersionTracking calls ParseVersionParts and immediately indexes into positions [0] and [1] without checking the slice length. ParseVersionParts can return fewer than 2 elements for single-component version strings (e.g., '5' returns []int{5}). IsValidSemver explicitly allows single-part versions (test case: 'valid 1 part', '1', true). This will panic with index-out-of-bounds.

Suggested fix: Check len(currentParts) >= 2 && len(lastParts) >= 2 before accessing [0] and [1].
title: ParseVersionParts index-out-of-bounds panic in CheckMetadataVersionTracking

description: wisps.go:325-343: getWisp acquires s.mu.RLock() at line 327 but does not use defer to release it. Instead, it manually calls RUnlock at three separate return points (lines 329, 334, 341). If scanIssueFromTable panics (e.g., nil pointer, index out of range), the RLock is never released, permanently deadlocking any writer trying to acquire the write lock. Suggested fix: restructure to use defer s.mu.RUnlock() or use a helper closure that defers the unlock.
title: getWisp RLock not deferred - panic causes permanent deadlock

description: Current: Each of 13 CLI tests calls git init (31s total). Solution: Use single test binary built once in init(), skip git operations where possible, or use mock filesystem.
title: Optimize CLI test suite - eliminate redundant git init calls

description:
Add messaging semantics and extended graph links to Beads, enabling it to serve as
the universal substrate for knowledge work - issues, messages, documents, and threads
as nodes in a queryable graph.

## Motivation

Gas Town (GGT) needs inter-agent communication. Rather than a separate mail system,
collapse messaging into Beads - one system, one sync, one query interface, all in git.

This also positions Beads as a foundation for:
- Company-wide issue tracking (like Notion)
- Threaded conversations (like Reddit/Slack)
- Knowledge graphs with loose associations
- Arbitrary workflow UIs built on top

## New Issue Type

**message** - ephemeral communication between workers
- sender: who sent it
- assignee: recipient
- priority: P0 (urgent) to P4 (routine)
- status: open (unread) -> closed (read)
- ephemeral: true = can be bulk-deleted after swarm

## New Graph Links

**replies_to** - conversation threading
- Messages reply to messages
- Enables Reddit-style nested threads
- Different from parent_id (not hierarchy, its conversation flow)

**relates_to** - loose see also associations
- Bidirectional knowledge graph edges
- Not blocking, not hierarchical, just related
- Enables discovery and traversal

**duplicates** - deduplication at scale
- Mark issue B as duplicate of canonical issue A
- Close B, link to A
- Essential for large issue databases

**supersedes** - version chains
- Design Doc v2 supersedes Design Doc v1
- Track evolution of artifacts

## New Fields (optional, any issue type)

- sender (string) - who created this (for messages)
- ephemeral (boolean) - can be bulk-deleted when closed

## New Commands

Messaging:
- bd mail send <recipient> -s Subject -m Body
- bd mail inbox (list open messages for me)
- bd mail read <id> (show message content)
- bd mail ack <id> (mark as read/close)
- bd mail reply <id> -m Response (reply to thread)

Graph links:
- bd relate <id1> <id2> (create relates_to link)
- bd duplicate <id> --of <canonical> (mark as duplicate)
- bd supersede <id> --with <new> (mark superseded)

Cleanup:
- bd cleanup --ephemeral (delete closed ephemeral issues)

## Identity Configuration

Workers need identity for sender field:
- BEADS_IDENTITY env var
- Or .beads/config.json: identity field

## Hooks (for GGT integration)

Beads as platform - extensible without knowing about GGT.
Hook files in .beads/hooks/:
- on_create (runs after bd create)
- on_update (runs after bd update)
- on_close (runs after bd close)
- on_message (runs after bd mail send)

GGT registers hooks to notify daemons of new messages.

## Schema Changes (Migration Required)

Add to issue schema:
- type: message (new valid type)
- sender: string (optional)
- ephemeral: boolean (optional)
- replies_to: string (issue ID, optional)
- relates_to: []string (issue IDs, optional)
- duplicates: string (canonical issue ID, optional)
- superseded_by: string (new issue ID, optional)

Migration adds fields as optional - existing beads unchanged.

## Success Criteria

1. bd mail send/inbox/read/ack/reply work end-to-end
2. replies_to creates proper thread structure
3. relates_to, duplicates, supersedes links queryable
4. Hooks fire on create/update/close/message
5. Identity configurable via env or config
6. Migration preserves all existing data
7. All new features have tests
title: Beads Messaging & Knowledge Graph (v0.30.2)

description:
Document all new features.

Files to update:
- README.md - brief mention of messaging capability
- AGENTS.md - update for AI agents using bd mail
- docs/messaging.md (new) - full messaging reference
- docs/graph-links.md (new) - graph link reference
- CHANGELOG.md - v0.30.2 release notes

Topics to cover:
- Mail commands with examples
- Graph link types and use cases
- Identity configuration
- Hooks setup for notifications
- Migration notes
title: Documentation for messaging and graph links

description: Tests in cmd/bd, internal/molecules, internal/storage/dolt, and internal/storage/dolt/compact fail because the test harness runs gt dolt start which fails with 'not in a Gas Town workspace' when run from a refinery rig worktree. Pre-existing issue.

When BEADS_TEST_MODE is set, the code skips the gt dolt start path and falls through to the generic error, letting test-managed servers handle their own lifecycle. All affected test packages pass: internal/storage/dolt, internal/molecules, cmd/bd.

close_reason: Completed with no code changes (already fixed or pushed directly to main)
description: Solo Beads users (no Gas Town, no Dogs) need built-in data lifecycle management. Three new bd subcommands: (1) bd gc — runs DECAY+COMPACT+GC in sequence (delete old closed issues, rebase commits, dolt gc), (2) bd compact — rebase old commits older than N days (default 30), (3) bd flatten — nuclear squash all history to one commit (Tim Sehn recipe: branch, soft-reset to initial, commit, swap main, gc). Auto-compact: bd could check last compact date on startup and suggest/auto-run if stale. This is critical for Beads adoption — without it, solo users hit storage limits. Sent briefing to bd-crew-emma.
notes:
Implemented three new top-level maintenance commands:

1. bd gc: Full lifecycle GC with three phases (decay/compact/dolt-gc). Decay deletes closed issues older than N days (default 90), compact phase reports commit count and suggests bd flatten, dolt gc phase runs dolt gc to reclaim space. Supports --dry-run, --force, --older-than, --skip-decay, --skip-dolt, --json.

2. bd compact: Squashes Dolt commits older than N days (default 30) into a single base commit, then cherry-picks recent commits on top. Uses dolt CLI operations (branch, checkout, reset --soft, cherry-pick). Supports --dry-run, --force, --days, --json.

3. bd flatten: Nuclear squash of ALL Dolt history into a single commit using the Tim Sehn recipe (branch, soft-reset to initial commit, commit, swap main, gc). Irreversible. Supports --dry-run, --force, --json.

All commands in the maint group. Removed deprecated compact alias (was forwarding to bd admin compact) to make room for the new Dolt commit compaction command.
title: bd gc / bd compact / bd flatten commands for standalone Beads

description: MEDIUM: mol_ready_gated.go (lines 112-113 and 127-128) uses Limit: 100 for gate filter and Limit: 500 for ready work filter. Results beyond these limits are silently truncated. Callers have no way to know results are incomplete. In large deployments these limits may be hit. Fix: remove limits, add pagination, or log warning when limit is hit.
title: findGateReadyMolecules hardcoded limits silently truncate results
