description:
In integrity.go line ~347, the code does storedRepoID[:8] and currentRepoID[:8] without checking that these strings are at least 8 characters long. If ComputeRepoID or GetMetadata returns a string shorter than 8 characters (e.g., corrupted metadata), this will panic with index-out-of-range. CheckRepoFingerprint also has no test coverage.

Suggested fix: Add length check before slicing, or use a safe truncation helper.
notes: Already fixed: truncateID helper was added in commit d1859a0d (bd-0jkc). CheckRepoFingerprint uses truncateID() at line 347 and 355. Tests exist: TestTruncateID covers empty/short/exact/long IDs, TestCheckRepoFingerprint_NoDatabase covers the no-database path. ComputeRepoID always returns 32 hex chars (sha256[:16] hex-encoded), so the practical panic risk was low, but the defense-in-depth fix is already in place.
title: Potential panic on short repo IDs with [:8] slicing in CheckRepoFingerprint

description:
In checks_nocgo.go, all nocgo stubs (lines 8-57) return StatusOK for checks that are actually skipped (CheckMergeArtifacts, CheckDuplicateIssues, CheckTestPollution, etc.). Returning 'ok' suggests the checks ran and found no problems. Users of nocgo builds will see an all-green report without realizing their installation has not been fully validated.

Similarly in migration_validation_nocgo.go, CheckMigrationReadiness returns StatusOK while result.Ready is false and result.Errors contains an error — contradictory.

Also, MigrationValidationResult struct diverges between cgo and nocgo: nocgo is missing ForeignPrefixCount and ForeignPrefixes fields, creating a latent compilation hazard.

Suggested fix: Return StatusWarning with 'Skipped: requires CGO' for nocgo stubs. Sync the struct fields.
title: Nocgo stubs return StatusOK for skipped checks, misleading users

close_reason: Fixed in 5c9c749a. Two-layer circuit breaker: witness blocks respawn at 3 attempts, spawn cap at 25 polecats.
description:
## Problem

The new re-sling functionality (witness DetectOrphanedBeads → RECOVERED_BEAD → deacon Redispatch → SpawnPolecatForSling) has no effective circuit breaker. When a task repeatedly kills its polecat, the witness-deacon feedback loop spawns a new polecat every 30-60 seconds indefinitely.

First incident: 2026-03-01, 162+ polecats spawned for beads rig on tasks bd-hn4q and bd-ttno. 150 claude processes, machine under heavy load. Deacon sent CRITICAL escalation, mayor killed all polecats manually.

## Existing Guards (and why they failed)

- Deacon 5-min cooldown per bead: witness patrol cycle is faster, sends fresh RECOVERED_BEAD mails
- Deacon max 3 redispatches: witness RECOVERED_BEAD bypasses deacon tracking (each mail is independent)
- Witness SPAWN_STORM warning at count > 2: informational only, does not block

## Fix (two layers)

### Layer 1: Per-bead backoff with hard stop (primary fix)
The witness already tracks respawn counts in bead-respawn-counts.json. Make it authoritative:
- Witness checks respawn count BEFORE sending RECOVERED_BEAD
- If count >= 3: do NOT send RECOVERED_BEAD, instead escalate directly to mayor
- Add exponential backoff between respawn attempts (30s, 2min, 10min)

### Layer 2: Rig-level polecat cap (safety net)
- SpawnPolecatForSling checks current polecat count before spawning
- Default cap: 25 per rig (configurable in rig config)
- If at cap: refuse to spawn, escalate
- This is a circuit breaker, not a capacity planner

## Key code locations
- Witness orphan detect: internal/witness/handlers.go:1676-1781
- Witness reset & mail: internal/witness/handlers.go:1603-1651
- Witness spawn counts: internal/witness/spawn_count.go
- Deacon redispatch: internal/deacon/redispatch.go:173-294
- Polecat spawn: internal/cmd/polecat_spawn.go:61-189
title: Polecat re-sling needs circuit breaker (spawn storm #22)

description: internal/hooks/hooks_test.go:365 TestRun_Async fails after 3.04s with 'Failed to read output file after retries: no such file or directory'. The async hook writes output to a file but the test retries are exhausted before the file appears. Fix by increasing retry timeout, fixing async file I/O ordering, or using a channel/waitgroup to synchronize instead of polling.
title: Fix hooks_test.go: TestRun_Async race condition on output file

description:
## Vision

Encode project-specific institutional knowledge into executable checks. CONTRIBUTING.md is documentation that's read once and forgotten; `bd preflight` is documentation that runs at exactly the right moment.

## Problem Statement

Contributors face a "last mile" problem - they do the work but stumble on project-specific gotchas at PR time:
- Nix vendorHash gets stale when go.sum changes
- Beads artifacts leak into PRs (see bd-umbf for namespace solution)
- Version mismatches between version.go and default.nix
- Tests/lint not run locally before pushing
- Other project-specific checks that only surface when CI fails

These are too obscure to remember, exist in docs nobody reads end-to-end, and waste CI round-trips.

## Why beads?

Beads already has a foothold in the contributor workflow. It knows:
- Git state (staged files, branch, dirty status)
- Project structure
- The specific issue being worked on
- Project-specific configuration

## Proposed Interface

### Tier 1: Checklist Mode (v1)

    $ bd preflight
    PR Readiness Checklist:

    [ ] Tests pass: go test -short ./...
    [ ] Lint passes: golangci-lint run ./...
    [ ] No beads pollution: check .beads/issues.jsonl diff
    [ ] Nix hash current: go.sum unchanged or vendorHash updated
    [ ] Version sync: version.go matches default.nix

    Run 'bd preflight --check' to validate automatically.

### Tier 2: Check Mode (v2)

    $ bd preflight --check
    ✓ Tests pass
    ✓ Lint passes
    ⚠ Beads pollution: 3 issues in diff - are these project issues or personal?
    ✗ Nix hash stale: go.sum changed, vendorHash needs update
      Fix: sha256-KRR6dXzsSw8OmEHGBEVDBOoIgfoZ2p0541T9ayjGHlI=
    ✓ Version sync

    1 error, 1 warning. Run 'bd preflight --fix' to auto-fix where possible.

### Tier 3: Fix Mode (v3)

    $ bd preflight --fix
    ✓ Updated vendorHash in default.nix
    ⚠ Cannot auto-fix beads pollution - manual review needed

## Checks to Implement

| Check | Description | Auto-fixable |
|-------|-------------|--------------|
| tests | Run go test -short ./... | No |
| lint | Run golangci-lint | Partial (gofmt) |
| beads-pollution | Detect personal issues in diff | No (see bd-umbf) |
| nix-hash | Detect stale vendorHash | Yes (if nix available) |
| version-sync | version.go matches default.nix | Yes |
| no-debug | No TODO/FIXME/console.log | Warn only |
| clean-stage | No unintended files staged | Warn only |

## Future: Configuration

Make checks configurable per-project via .beads/preflight.yaml:

    preflight:
      checks:
        - name: tests
          run: go test -short ./...
          required: true
        - name: no-secrets
          pattern: "**/*.env"
          staged: deny
        - name: custom-check
          run: ./scripts/validate.sh

This lets any project using beads define their own preflight checks.

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Static Checklist
- Implement bd preflight with hardcoded checklist for beads
- No execution, just prints what to check
- Update CONTRIBUTING.md to reference it

### Phase 2: Automated Checks
- Implement bd preflight --check
- Run tests, lint, detect stale hashes
- Clear pass/fail/warn output

### Phase 3: Auto-fix
- Implement bd preflight --fix
- Fix vendorHash, version sync
- Integrate with bd-umbf solution for pollution

### Phase 4: Configuration
- .beads/preflight.yaml support
- Make it useful for other projects using beads
- Plugin/hook system for custom checks

## Dependencies

- bd-umbf: Namespace isolation for beads pollution (blocking for full solution)

## Success Metrics

- Fewer CI failures on first PR push
- Reduced "fix nix hash" commits
- Contributors report preflight caught issues before CI
title: bd preflight: PR readiness checks for contributors

description:
PR #551 now persists close_reason to the database, but there's a question about whether this field should be exported to JSONL format.

## Current State
- close_reason is stored in issues.close_reason column
- close_reason is also stored in events table (audit trail)
- The JSONL export format may or may not include close_reason

## Questions
1. Should close_reason be exported to JSONL format?
2. If yes, where should it go (root level or nested in events)?
3. Should there be any special handling to avoid duplication?
4. How should close_reason be handled during JSONL import?

## Why This Matters
- JSONL is the git-friendly sync format
- Other beads instances import from JSONL
- close_reason is meaningful data that should be preserved across clones

## Suggested Action
- Check if close_reason is currently exported in JSONL
- If not, add it to the export schema
- Document the field in JSONL format spec
- Add tests for round-trip (export -> import -> verify close_reason)
title: Add close_reason to JSONL export format documentation

description: In cmd/bd/dolt.go doltIdleMonitorCmd (line 363), the PID file is written immediately but only cleaned up in the SIGTERM/SIGINT handler (line 383). If the monitor exits normally (e.g., idle timeout disabled at line 371 with early return, or RunIdleMonitor returns normally at line 387), the PID file is left behind as an orphan. The early return at line 371 when idle timeout is disabled ('0') exits without cleaning up the PID file it just wrote on line 363. Should defer PID file cleanup.

description:
Multiple check functions construct paths relative to cwd instead of accepting a path parameter or resolving the project root:

1. gitignore.go line ~91: CheckGitignore uses filepath.Join('.beads', '.gitignore')
2. gitignore.go: CheckRedirectNotTracked, CheckLastTouchedNotTracked, FixRedirectTracking, FixLastTouchedTracking all use relative paths
3. claude.go lines ~111-112, 175-176, 222-223: isBeadsPluginInstalled, isMCPServerInstalled, hasClaudeHooks use filepath.Join('.claude', 'settings.json')

If bd doctor is run from a subdirectory of the project, these checks will look in the wrong location. In contrast, functions like CheckConfigValues correctly take an explicit repoPath parameter.

Suggested fix: Accept a path parameter or resolve the project root internally, consistent with other checks.
title: Relative path usage makes doctor checks fragile when run from subdirectories

description:
In git.go line ~687, when provider.GetOpenIssues(ctx) fails, FindOrphanedIssues returns ([]OrphanIssue{}, nil) — no error. This silently hides database connectivity failures, authentication issues, or other real errors. The caller CheckOrphanedIssues would report 'no orphans' when the check actually could not run.

Also, CheckGitWorkingTree and CheckGitUpstream (lines ~282-293, 383-431) run multiple exec.Command('git', ...) calls without a context timeout. If the git process hangs, the doctor check blocks indefinitely.

Suggested fix: Return the error from GetOpenIssues. Use exec.CommandContext with a timeout for git commands.
title: FindOrphanedIssues silently swallows provider errors

description:
In autoimport.go:170-172, YAML parsing errors are silently ignored. If a user has malformed YAML in config.yaml, sync-branch will just silently be empty with no feedback.

Recommendation: Add debug logging since this function is only called during auto-import, and debugging silent failures is painful.

Add: debug.Logf("Warning: failed to parse config.yaml: %v", err)
title: getLocalSyncBranch silently ignores YAML parse errors

description: In store.go Push() (line 1299), ForcePush() (1335), and Pull() (1372), the SSH check (isSSHRemote) is only reached if remoteUser is empty. When remoteUser is set, the code goes directly to CALL DOLT_PUSH/PULL with --user flag via SQL. But if a Hosted Dolt remote also uses SSH transport (git+ssh://), the SQL procedure will still time out because the MySQL connection drops before SSH transfer completes — the exact problem the SSH fallback was designed to prevent. The SSH check should happen BEFORE the remoteUser check, or the remoteUser path should also use CLI for SSH URLs. This could cause silent push/pull failures for Hosted Dolt users with SSH remotes.
title: Push/ForcePush/Pull: SSH fallback skipped when remoteUser is set for Hosted Dolt SSH remotes

description: ephemeral_routing.go:251-262: PromoteFromEphemeral copies events and comments from wisp tables to permanent tables using '_, _ = s.execContext(...)' — both result and error are discarded. Then line 265 calls deleteWisp which removes the wisp and all wisp_* auxiliary records. If the copy fails (e.g., connection error, constraint violation), the promotion completes successfully but events/comments are permanently lost. Suggested fix: at minimum log the error; ideally make event/comment copy a required step or warn the user if data was lost during promotion.
title: PromoteFromEphemeral discards event/comment copy errors

description:
> bd sync --no-daemon
→ Exporting pending changes to JSONL...
→ Committing changes to sync branch 'beads-sync'...
Error committing to sync branch: failed to create worktree: failed to create worktree parent directory: mkdir /var/home/matt/dev/beads/fix-ci/.git: not a directory
notes:
**Problem Diagnosed**: The `bd sync` command was failing with "mkdir /var/home/matt/dev/beads/fix-ci/.git: not a directory" because it was being executed from the wrong directory.

**Root Cause**: The command was run from `/var/home/matt/dev/beads` (where the `fix-ci` worktree exists) instead of the main repository directory `/var/home/matt/dev/beads/main`. Since `fix-ci` is a git worktree with a `.git` file (not directory), the worktree creation logic failed when trying to create `<current_dir>/.git/beads-worktrees/<branch>`.

**Solution Verified**: Execute `bd sync` from the main repository directory:
```bash
cd main && bd sync --dry-run
```
title: Error committing to sync branch: failed to create worktree

description:
## Problem

When ensureDatabaseFresh() calls CheckStaleness() and it errors (corrupted metadata, permission issues, etc.), we silently proceed with potentially stale data.

**Location:** cmd/bd/staleness.go:27-32

**Scenarios:**
- Corrupted metadata table
- Database locked by another process  
- Permission issues reading JSONL file
- Invalid last_import_time format in DB

## Current Code

```go
isStale, err := autoimport.CheckStaleness(ctx, store, dbPath)
if err \!= nil {
    // If we can't determine staleness, allow operation to proceed
    // (better to show potentially stale data than block user)
    return nil
}
```

## Fix

```go
isStale, err := autoimport.CheckStaleness(ctx, store, dbPath)
if err \!= nil {
    fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "Warning: Could not verify database freshness: %v\n", err)
    fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "Proceeding anyway. Data may be stale.\n\n")
    return nil
}
```

## Impact
Medium - users should know when staleness check fails

## Effort
Easy - 5 minutes
title: Add warning when staleness check errors

close_reason: Fixed: added rows.Err() checks after all for-rows.Next() loops (~20 locations)
description:
Multiple functions iterate sql.Rows with rows.Next() but never check rows.Err() after the loop. Per Go database/sql docs, rows.Next() can return false due to an iteration error, not just end-of-results. Without checking rows.Err(), database errors during iteration are silently lost and partial results are treated as complete.

Affected locations:
- validation.go: checkOrphanedDependenciesDB (~line 176), checkChildParentDependenciesDB (~line 449)
- deep.go: checkParentConsistency, checkDependencyIntegrity, checkEpicCompleteness, checkAgentBeadIntegrity, checkMailThreadIntegrity, checkMoleculeIntegrity (6 functions)
- dolt.go: checkStatusWithDB (~line 387), checkPhantomDatabases (~line 558)
- server.go: checkStaleDatabases (~line 211), checkDatabaseExists (~line 401)
- migration_validation.go: checkDoltLocks (~line 481), categorizeDoltExtras (~line 527)
- perf_dolt.go: measureQueryTime (~line 222)
- integrity.go: CheckIDFormat (~line 67)
- fix/validation.go: OrphanedDependencies (~line 154), ChildParentDependencies (~line 238)

Suggested fix: Add 'if err := rows.Err(); err != nil { return err }' after each for-rows.Next() loop. Note that maintenance.go correctly does this at line 615 — use that as a reference pattern.
title: Systematic missing rows.Err() checks after SQL row iteration (~14+ locations)

description: Per the design document bd-zvg Open Question 1: Should resurrection log a warning? Recommendation was Yes. Currently, when an expired tombstone loses to a live issue (resurrection), there is no logging or debugging output. This makes it hard to understand why an issue reappeared. Recommendation: Add optional debug logging when resurrection occurs, e.g., Issue bd-abc resurrected (tombstone expired). Files: internal/merge/merge.go:359-366, 371-378, 400-405, 410-415
title: No logging/debugging for tombstone resurrection events

description: GetNewlyUnblockedByClose (dependencies.go:809) has nested subquery with 2-table JOIN + correlated NOT EXISTS. Most dangerous remaining query pattern that could crash/hang Dolt. Rewrite to avoid nested JOIN. Also review cycle detection CTE (dependencies.go:52). See hq-g4nxe for full audit.
notes:
Analysis complete:

1. GetNewlyUnblockedByClose (line 822): REWRITTEN. Decomposed nested JOIN + correlated NOT EXISTS into two sequential queries:
   - Query 1: Find candidate issues blocked by the closed issue (simple JOIN)
   - Query 2: Check which candidates still have other open blockers (simple JOIN with IN clause)
   - Go-side filtering removes still-blocked candidates
   Both existing tests pass.

2. Cycle detection CTE (line 52): REVIEWED, safe. Simple recursive CTE with:
   - Depth limit of 100 (prevents runaway recursion)
   - Single table JOIN per recursion (dependencies only)
   - No nested JOINs or correlated subqueries
   - Runs within a transaction
   This is a standard graph-reachability pattern that Dolt handles well.
title: SQL audit: rewrite GetNewlyUnblockedByClose nested JOIN subquery

description: ephemeral_routing.go:15 uses strings.Contains(id, '-wisp-') for routing decisions. Breaks for explicit-ID ephemerals (GH#2053). Workarounds exist (isActiveWisp, partitionByWispStatus, batchWispExists) but the fundamental pattern of routing by ID substring creates a fragile dual-path that every new storage method must remember to handle. Consider a column-based approach (check ephemeral flag in DB) as the canonical routing.
title: IsEphemeralID routes by ID substring '-wisp-' - fragile convention
Improve CheckStaleness error handling

If a wisp has a dependency pointing to a renamed issue (e.g., wisp_dependencies.depends_on_id = oldID), that reference becomes dangling after the rename. description:
BEGIN IMMEDIATE fails immediately on SQLITE_BUSY instead of retrying with exponential backoff.

Location: internal/storage/sqlite/sqlite.go:223-225

Problem:
- Under concurrent write load, BEGIN IMMEDIATE can fail with SQLITE_BUSY
- Current implementation fails immediately instead of retrying
- Results in spurious failures under normal concurrent usage

Solution: Implement exponential backoff retry:
- Retry up to N times (e.g., 5)
- Backoff: 10ms, 20ms, 40ms, 80ms, 160ms
- Check for context cancellation between retries
- Only retry on SQLITE_BUSY/database locked errors

Impact: Spurious failures under concurrent write load

Effort: 3 hours
title: Implement transaction retry logic for SQLITE_BUSY

description: Review commit 49aed46c in beads repo. Check: migration safety guards, old-schema compatibility, doctor recovery logic, error handling.
design:
## Code Review: SQLite-to-Dolt Migration Safety (49aed46c)

### Summary
Well-structured, safety-first migration PR. 18 files, ~3200 lines added, 117+ tests. The four review areas are analyzed below.

---

### 1. Migration Safety Guards — GOOD with one weakness

**backupSQLite**: Solid. O_EXCL prevents TOCTOU races, 0600 permissions, counter-based collision handling up to 100 retries.

**verifyServerTarget**: WEAK CHECK. Despite the name, it cannot actually prevent writing to the wrong project's Dolt server. It only catches: (a) non-MySQL services on the port, (b) unreachable servers with non-ECONNREFUSED errors. If another project's Dolt server is on the same port, this check passes — database doesn't exist yet = 'safe'. The function always returns nil for the normal first-migration case. Recommend: at minimum, add a comment acknowledging this limitation. Better: check for unexpected databases that indicate a different project's server.

**verifyMigrationCounts**: Correct. Uses >= semantics for idempotent re-runs.

**finalizeMigration**: Good overall. Comment says 'atomic metadata update' but it's not truly atomic — crash between cfg.Save() and os.Rename() leaves metadata pointing to dolt while beads.db still exists. MITIGATED: doctor CheckBrokenMigrationState detects and fixes this exact scenario. Recommend: update comment to say 'near-atomic' and reference the doctor fix.

**rollbackMetadata**: Simple and correct.

---

### 2. Old-Schema Compatibility — GOOD

Both CGO (migrate_dolt.go) and shim (migrate_shim.go) extract paths updated consistently with identical column lists using the DRY opt() helper.

Good: created_by and owner changed from direct scan to sql.NullString scan for old schemas where these columns may not exist.

Minor issue: migrate_dolt.go comment says 'Core columns (id, title, status, priority, created_at, updated_at) are assumed present in all versions' but then uses opt('title', '""') — making title optional contradicts the comment. Comment should be corrected or removed.

Tested against 12 real-world databases spanning multiple schema versions — strong validation.

---

### 3. Doctor Recovery Logic — GOOD

**CheckBrokenMigrationState**: Properly aware of server mode (no false positive when dolt/ dir absent). Checks both beads.db and beads.db.migrated as recovery sources. Distinguishes recoverable vs unrecoverable states.

**CheckSQLiteResidue**: Guards against renaming active SQLite database (checks backend first). Handles zero-byte files correctly.

**CheckEmbeddedModeConcurrency**: Checks dolt-access.lock and noms LOCK files. Appropriately uses warnings not errors.

**fix/BrokenMigrationState**: Re-verifies conditions before applying (defense in depth). Handles .migrated file recovery. Sets database='beads.db' correctly for SQLite backend.

**fix/SQLiteResidue**: Guards against overwriting existing .migrated file. Backend guard prevents accidental rename on SQLite backend.

---

### 4. Error Handling — GOOD

Every failure point in migrate_auto.go and migrate_shim.go includes the backup path in error messages — users always know where their data is.

Rollback on verification failure properly removes dolt dir + restores metadata. Import failure cleanup removes the dolt directory that was just created.

main.go SQLite backend guard provides clear error message with migration instructions and BEADS_BACKEND override as escape hatch.

configfile.go GetBackend priority chain (env > config > default) is correct with case-insensitive normalization and unrecognized-value warnings.

Minor: migrate_auto.go has redundant doltPath assignment (computed twice to same value). Harmless.

Minor: shim path uses cfg.GetDoltServerPort() directly while auto path uses doltserver.DefaultConfig(). These could diverge if GetDoltServerPort() returns 0, but verifyServerTarget handles port==0 by returning nil, so safe.

---

### Issues Found (non-blocking)

1. **verifyServerTarget is weak** — cannot detect wrong-project Dolt servers (design limitation, mitigated by manual inspection)
2. **Comment/code mismatch** in migrate_dolt.go re 'core columns assumed present' vs all columns using opt()
3. **Redundant doltPath assignment** in migrate_auto.go (harmless)
4. **'Atomic' finalization isn't truly atomic** — mitigated by doctor fix

### Verdict: APPROVE with minor suggestions
No blocking issues. The safety model (backup-first, verify-then-finalize, doctor-recoverable) is sound. Test coverage is comprehensive at 117+ tests across unit and integration.
notes: Code review complete. Reviewed all 18 files in commit 49aed46c. Verdict: APPROVE with minor suggestions. Four non-blocking issues found (verifyServerTarget weakness, comment/code mismatch, redundant assignment, non-atomic finalization). The safety model is sound: backup-first, verify-then-finalize, doctor-recoverable. 117+ tests provide comprehensive coverage.
title: Code review: SQLite-to-Dolt migration safety (49aed46c)

description:
Comprehensive code review of the merged context engineering PR (PR #481) that reduces MCP context usage by 80-90%.

## Summary
The PR successfully implements lazy tool schema loading and minimal issue models to reduce context window usage. Overall implementation is solid and well-tested.

## Positive Findings
✅ Well-designed models (IssueMinimal, CompactedResult)
✅ Comprehensive test coverage (28 tests, all passing)
✅ Clear documentation and comments
✅ Backward compatibility preserved (show() still returns full Issue)
✅ Sensible defaults (COMPACTION_THRESHOLD=20, PREVIEW_COUNT=5)
✅ Tool catalog complete with all 15 tools documented

## Issues Identified
See linked issues for specific followup tasks.

## Context Engineering Architecture
- discover_tools(): List tool names only (~500 bytes vs ~15KB)
- get_tool_info(name): Get specific tool details on-demand
- IssueMinimal: Lightweight model for list views (~80 bytes vs ~400 bytes)
- CompactedResult: Auto-compacts results with >20 issues
- _to_minimal(): Conversion function (efficient, no N+1 issues)
title: Code Review: PR #481 - Context Engineering Optimizations

description:
PR #481 changed the return types of `ready()` and `list()` from `list[Issue]` to `list[IssueMinimal] | CompactedResult`. This is a breaking change for MCP clients.

## Impact Assessment
Breaking change affects:
- Any MCP client expecting `list[Issue]` from ready()
- Any MCP client expecting `list[Issue]` from list()
- Client code that accesses full Issue fields (description, design, acceptance_criteria, timestamps, dependencies, dependents)

## Current Behavior
- ready() returns `list[IssueMinimal] | CompactedResult`
- list() returns `list[IssueMinimal] | CompactedResult`
- show() still returns full `Issue` (good)

## Considerations
**Pros of current approach:**
- Forces clients to use show() for full details (good for context efficiency)
- Simple mental model (always use show for full data)
- Documentation warns about this

**Cons:**
- Clients expecting list[Issue] will break
- No graceful degradation option
- No migration period

## Potential Solutions
1. Add optional parameter `full_details=false` to ready/list (would increase payload)
2. Create separate tools: ready_minimal/list_minimal + ready_full/list_full
3. Accept breaking change and document upgrade path (current approach)
4. Version the MCP server and document migration guide

## Recommendation
Current approach (solution 3) is reasonable if:
- Changelog clearly documents the breaking change
- Migration guide provided to clients
- Error handling is graceful for clients expecting specific fields
title: Consider backwards compatibility for ready() and list() return type change

description:
In fix/database_integrity.go sqliteIntegrityRecovery (lines ~113-117), if getBdBinary() fails, the function returns the error but does NOT restore the backup. The database file has already been moved at line ~103. Compare with doltIntegrityRecovery (lines ~58-61) which correctly restores the backup on getBdBinary() failure.

This leaves users with no database file at the expected path if getBdBinary() fails during SQLite recovery.

Suggested fix: Restore backupDB back to dbPath before returning the error, matching the Dolt recovery pattern.
title: fix/database_integrity.go: SQLite recovery does not restore backup on getBdBinary() failure

description:

Reference: https://www.dolthub.com/blog/2024-01-03-announcing-dolt-rebase/

description:
migrate_dolt_nocgo.go rejects 'bd migrate --to-dolt' with 'Dolt backend requires CGO' error. However, migrate_shim.go already provides a working SQLite extraction path via the system sqlite3 CLI that works without CGO. The auto-migration path (migrate_auto_nocgo.go) correctly delegates to shimMigrateSQLiteToDolt(), but the explicit --to-dolt path does not.

This means users with nocgo builds who want to explicitly migrate via 'bd migrate --to-dolt' are blocked, even though the auto-migration works fine on the same binary.

Fix: In migrate_dolt_nocgo.go, delegate to a shim-based migration function instead of rejecting outright. Use the same sqlite3 CLI extraction + importToDolt path that the auto-migration uses.
title: bd migrate --to-dolt blocked in nocgo builds unnecessarily

description: credentials.go:47-53: encryptionKey() derives AES-256 key from SHA256(dbPath + hardcoded-salt). The dbPath is predictable (e.g., ~/.beads/.dolt), making the key guessable. Anyone with knowledge of the file path can decrypt stored federation passwords. The salt is a hardcoded string, not a random value. Additionally, moving the database to a different path silently breaks all encrypted credentials. Suggested fix: use a randomly-generated key stored securely (e.g., in a separate file with restrictive permissions, or system keyring), or at minimum add a per-installation random salt.
notes: Fix: replaced SHA256(dbPath+salt) key derivation with random 32-byte key stored in .encryption_key file (0600 perms). Added migration to re-encrypt existing credentials. Key file location: parent dir of dbPath (the .beads dir).
title: Encryption key derivation from dbPath is predictable

1. OrphanedDependencies and ChildParentDependencies fix operations can hang indefinitely if Dolt server is unresponsive (no timeout)

Suggested fix: Unify openDoltDB and openFixDB into a single function with timeout and password support.

(2) Migrated migrations_test.go to shared server + branch-per-test isolation via new TestMain with issues table committed to main. Eliminated 8 per-test server spawns. description:
During swarm operations, polecats frequently get stuck waiting for interactive prompts from shell commands like:
- cp prompting 'overwrite file? (y/n)'
- mv prompting 'overwrite file? (y/n)'  
- rm prompting 'remove file?'

This happens because macOS aliases or shell configs may have -i flags set by default.

Root cause: Claude Code runs commands that trigger interactive confirmation prompts, but cannot respond to them, causing the agent to hang indefinitely.

Observed in: Multiple polecats during GH issues swarm (Dec 2024)
- Derrick, Roustabout, Prospector, Warboy all got stuck on y/n prompts

Suggested fixes:
1. AGENTS.md should instruct agents to always use -f flag with cp/mv/rm
2. Polecat startup could set shell aliases to use non-interactive versions
3. bd prime hook could include guidance about non-interactive commands
4. Consider detecting stuck prompts and auto-recovering
notes: Implemented: PreToolUse hook (.claude/hooks/block-interactive-cmds.sh) that blocks cp/mv/rm without -f flag, preventing interactive prompt hangs from macOS -i aliases. Also registered hook in settings.json and added non-interactive guidance to default agents.md template.
title: Polecats get stuck on interactive shell prompts (cp/mv/rm -i)

description: In internal/testutil/fixtures/fixtures.go:467, importFromJSONL checks for 'UNIQUE constraint failed' which is a SQLite error message. Dolt (MySQL-compatible) reports duplicate key violations as 'Duplicate entry' (MySQL error 1062). This check never matches on Dolt, so duplicate-ID errors propagate as failures instead of being silently skipped. Fix: change the error string match to also check for 'Duplicate entry' or use MySQL error code 1062.
title: Stale SQLite error check in fixtures importFromJSONL

close_reason: Shipped ac78ec96: flock-based test server coordination, 3-file split (common/unix/windows)
description:
45 zombie dolt sql-server processes found on random high ports (13000-14300). Root cause: race condition in beads/mayor/rig/internal/testutil/testdoltserver.go lines 120-157.

FindFreePort() binds to :0, gets OS port, closes listener. Between close and dolt binding, concurrent test grabs that port. serverCmd.Start() succeeds but dolt fails to bind. WaitForServer() hangs 30s, then cleanup kills one process -- but with 4-5 parallel packages x 3 retries x 30s timeouts = 45 zombies in 3 minutes.

Cleanup failures: PID file written after Start() (references dead process on race), CleanStaleTestServers() only runs on next invocation, SIGKILL skips defers entirely.

Fix: port gastown's lock-based approach from internal/testutil/doltserver_unix.go -- sync.Once + syscall.Flock() for cross-process port coordination. Same pattern exists in multiple crew variants (collins, darcy, emma, etc.) which all have copies of testdoltserver.go.

Reference: gastown/mayor/rig/internal/testutil/doltserver_unix.go for the working pattern.
notes: Implemented: Ported gastown flock-based approach to beads testdoltserver.go. Split into 3 files: common (testdoltserver.go), unix (testdoltserver_unix.go with syscall.Flock), windows fallback (testdoltserver_windows.go). Uses sync.Once for in-process singleton + LOCK_EX/LOCK_SH for cross-process coordination. Added reapStaleDoltServers to catch zombies from SIGKILL'd processes. API surface unchanged - all callers work without modification. All test packages pass.
title: Fix test server zombie leak: port allocation race in testdoltserver.go

description: Pinned beads (e.g. bd-pr-sheriff) show up in open issue listings. Pinned is a distinct lifecycle state — these beads are permanent references, not actionable work. bd list --status=open should exclude pinned beads, or pinned should be a separate filter.
title: Pinned beads appear in bd list --status=open

description:
## Problem

When contributors work on beads-the-project using beads-the-tool, their personal work-tracking issues leak into PRs. The .beads/issues.jsonl is intentionally tracked (it's the project's issue database), but contributors' local issues pollute the diff.

This is a recursion problem unique to self-hosting projects.

## Possible Solutions to Explore

1. **Contributor namespaces** - Each contributor gets a private prefix (e.g., `bd-steve-xxxx`) that's gitignored or filtered
2. **Separate database** - Contributors use BEADS_DIR pointing elsewhere for personal tracking
3. **Issue ownership/visibility flags** - Mark issues as "local-only" vs "project"
4. **Prefix-based filtering** - Configure which prefixes are committed vs ignored

## Design Considerations

- Should be zero-friction for contributors (no manual setup)
- Must not break existing workflows
- Needs to work with sync/collaboration features
- Consider: what if a "personal" issue graduates to "project" issue?

## Expansion Needed

This is a placeholder. Needs detailed design exploration before implementation.
title: Design contributor namespace isolation for beads pollution prevention

description:
In federation.go:

1. Line ~112: remotesAPIPort is hardcoded to 8080. If the user configured a different remotesapi port, the check reports the wrong port as inaccessible. The port should be read from config, similar to how the SQL server port is derived via doltserver.DefaultConfig.

2. Line ~71: CheckFederationRemotesAPI looks for PID file at filepath.Join(doltPath, 'dolt-server.pid') (inside the dolt data directory), but the PID file is typically at filepath.Join(beadsDir, 'dolt-server.pid') as seen in gitignore.go line 42. Wrong path means the check incorrectly reports server as not running.

3. No test coverage exists for any federation checks.

Suggested fix: Read port from config. Fix PID file path. Add tests.
title: Federation checks: hardcoded port 8080 and possible wrong PID file path

description: transaction.go:182-261: doltTransaction.SearchIssues supports only 5 filter fields (query text, ParentID, Status, SpecIDPrefix, SourceRepo). The store-level DoltStore.SearchIssues supports ~25+ filter fields (Labels, Assignee, Priority, IssueType, MolType, WispType, date ranges, Pinned, NoAssignee, etc.). This means the same IssueFilter struct produces different results inside vs outside a transaction, which is a subtle API contract violation. Callers using RunInTransaction with complex filters will silently get unfiltered results. Suggested fix: align the transaction-level filter support with the store-level, or at minimum return an error if unsupported filters are passed.
title: Transaction SearchIssues supports far fewer filters than store-level

description: bd show only queries the issues table but wisps are stored in the wisps table (via wispIssueTable() routing). When refinery or other agents try to bd show a wisp ID, it returns 'not found'. This blocks refinery patrol hook/report operations. Root cause identified by gastown/refinery. Fix: bd show (and related lookup functions like GetIssue) need to check wispIssueTable() routing — if the ID has a wisp pattern, query the wisps table. See beads internal/storage/dolt/wisps.go for the routing logic.
notes: Implemented fix: 6 storage functions now route wisp lookups through wisps/wisp_dependencies tables instead of hardcoding issues/dependencies. Key changes in queries.go (GetMoleculeProgress, computeBlockedIDs, GetBlockedIssues, GetEpicsEligibleForClosure), dependencies.go (AddDependency target check), and transaction.go (SearchIssues ephemeral ID routing). All tests pass.
title: bd show can't find wisps: only queries issues table, not wisps table

queries.go:16-34: SearchIssues calls searchWisps and if it returns an error, silently falls through to query the issues table instead. The error is completely discarded. This was added for backwards compatibility with pre-migration databases, but it masks real errors (query timeouts, connection failures, corrupt data). A timeout on the wisps query would result in silently returning stale/wrong results from the issues table. Suggested fix: distinguish between 'table does not exist' errors (legitimate fallthrough) and real errors (should be returned). Same pattern at lines 29-34 for allEphemeral(filter.IDs).
title: SearchIssues ephemeral fallthrough silently swallows errors

description:
In legacy.go, all 15+ DoctorCheck structs use raw string literals ('ok', 'warning') for the Status field instead of StatusOK, StatusWarning, StatusError constants from types.go. If these constants are ever changed, legacy.go checks will produce incorrect status values.

Also, types.go defines Status as a raw string with no type safety. Using a typed string (type Status string) would provide compile-time checking.

Suggested fix: Replace all string literals with constants. Consider adding a typed Status.
title: Hardcoded status strings in legacy.go bypass StatusOK/StatusWarning constants

description: In credentials.go withPeerCredentials (line 283), when a peer has no credentials (peer.Username and peer.Password are both empty), the code skips the mutex and runs fn() without any locking. But if another goroutine concurrently runs withPeerCredentials WITH credentials, the env vars could be set while this no-credential fn() is executing. The no-credential path's dolt CLI operations (if any) would inherit stale or concurrent credential env vars. The mutex should protect all federation operations, not just ones with explicit credentials.
title: withPeerCredentials: continues without mutex when peer has no credentials

description: In credentials.go encryptionKey() (line 47), the AES-256 key is derived from SHA-256(dbPath + 'beads-federation-key-v1'). This means: (1) anyone who knows the dbPath can derive the key and decrypt stored passwords — the path is not a secret, (2) if the database directory is moved/renamed, all stored federation credentials become unreadable, (3) the static salt 'beads-federation-key-v1' provides no real entropy. The comment acknowledges this ('For production, consider using system keyring or external secret managers') but this should be tracked. Stored credentials in the federation_peers table are effectively obfuscated, not encrypted.
title: credentials.go: encryption key derived from dbPath is weak and portable

description: Rebuilt gt: 9332de3a → 8d20a6a9 (+2 commits)
title: Plugin run: rebuild-gt

description: In store.go doltCLIPush (line 1259) and doltCLIPull (1276), exec.CommandContext is used with the caller's context. However, Push() and Pull() create their context from the incoming ctx which may have no deadline. If the SSH transfer hangs (network issue, DNS, SSH key prompt), the CLI process will block forever. The SQL path has MySQL driver timeouts, but the CLI path has none. Should add a timeout context (e.g., 5 minutes) wrapping the exec to prevent indefinite hangs. This is the exact scenario the CloseWithTimeout pattern in doltutil was designed for.
title: doltCLIPush/doltCLIPull: no timeout on CLI exec, can hang indefinitely

notes: Implemented bd export command with -o, --all, --include-infra, --scrub flags. Exports JSONL with labels, dependencies, counts. Includes wisps. 7 tests pass (file, stdout, scrub, round-trip, pollution filter). Registered as read-only command.

description: dependencies.go:68-87: AddDependency's cycle detection CTE only traverses the 'dependencies' table. Cross-table cycles involving wisp_dependencies are not detected. For example: permanent issue A depends on wisp B (in wisp_dependencies), wisp B depends on permanent issue A (in dependencies) — this cycle is not caught. Similarly, DetectCycles (line 694) only queries GetAllDependencyRecords which only reads the permanent dependencies table. Suggested fix: extend the cycle detection CTE to UNION both dependency tables, or run the check against both tables.
notes: Three fixes needed: (1) AddDependency CTE at dependencies.go:68 only JOINs dependencies table - needs UNION ALL with wisp_dependencies. (2) addWispDependency at wisps.go:887 has NO cycle detection at all - needs same unified CTE. (3) DetectCycles at dependencies.go:694 calls GetAllDependencyRecords which only reads dependencies table - needs to also query wisp_dependencies and merge into graph.
title: Cycle detection doesn't span wisp_dependencies table

description:
Implement a --fix flag for bd doctor that can automatically repair detected issues.

Requirements:
- Add --fix flag to bd doctor command
- Show all fixable issues and prompt for confirmation before applying fixes
- Organize fix implementations under doctor/fix/<type_of_fix>.go
- Each fix type should have its own file (e.g., doctor/fix/hooks.go, doctor/fix/sync.go)
- Display what will be fixed and ask user to confirm (Y/n) before proceeding
- Support fixing issues like:
  - Missing or broken git hooks
  - Sync problems with remote
  - File permission issues
  - Any other auto-repairable issues doctor detects

Implementation notes:
- Maintain separation between detection (existing doctor code) and repair (new fix code)
- Each fix should be idempotent and safe to run multiple times
- Provide clear output about what was fixed
- Log any fixes that fail with actionable error messages
title: Add bd doctor --fix flag to automatically repair issues

description: In cmd/bd/dolt.go doltCleanDatabasesCmd (line 525), database names from SHOW DATABASES are interpolated into a DROP DATABASE statement using backtick quoting: fmt.Sprintf('DROP DATABASE `%s`', name). If a database name contains a backtick character, this could break out of the quoted identifier. While database names from SHOW DATABASES are typically safe, Dolt allows creating databases with unusual names. The nolint:gosec comment acknowledges the G201 warning but the defense ('name from SHOW DATABASES') isn't sufficient — should escape backticks within the name (replace ` with ``) or use a parameterized approach.
title: dolt clean-databases: SQL injection via backtick-quoted database names from SHOW DATABASES

description:
Code review of PR #551 which fixes close_reason persistence bug.

## Summary
The PR correctly fixes a bug where close_reason was only stored in the events table, not in the issues.close_reason column. This caused `bd show --json` to return empty close_reason.

description:
Standalone bd users need a simple backup story. Add 'bd backup' command that wraps Dolt backup operations:

- bd backup init <path> — set up a filesystem backup destination (wraps dolt backup add)
- bd backup sync — push to configured backup (wraps dolt backup sync)
- bd backup restore <path> — restore from a backup
- bd backup status — show last backup time, destination, size

Also document DoltHub as the recommended cloud remote path for solo users. Git-protocol remotes (git+ssh to GitHub) are not viable — 20+ min push times, unbounded git-remote-cache growth, force-push required after any recovery.

Target audience: solo devs using bd outside Gas Town who want their issues backed up somewhere safe (external drive, NAS, Dropbox, or DoltHub).
notes: Implemented: bd backup init <path>, bd backup sync, enhanced bd backup status with Dolt backup info and database size. Wraps DOLT_BACKUP SQL procedures for standalone user backups to filesystem/DoltHub.
title: bd backup command for standalone users

description:
config.go:27 Long description says 'Configuration is stored per-project in .beads/*.db and is version-control-friendly.' The '.beads/*.db' reference is from the SQLite era. With Dolt as the only backend, config is stored in the Dolt database (accessed via sql-server), not a .db file.

Fix: Update the Long description to say something like 'Configuration is stored per-project in the beads database and is version-control-friendly.'
title: Stale SQLite reference in config.go help text

description: In federation.go, PushTo (line 16), PullFrom (30), and Fetch (51) always use CALL DOLT_PUSH/PULL/FETCH via SQL, with no SSH fallback. If a federation peer uses an SSH remote URL, these operations will time out the same way store.go's Push/Pull do without the SSH fallback. The store.go methods (Push, Pull, ForcePush) have SSH detection via isSSHRemote() and fall back to CLI (doltCLIPush/doltCLIPull), but federation.go does not replicate this pattern. Federation operations with SSH peers will silently hang or timeout.
title: federation PushTo/PullFrom/Fetch: missing SSH fallback for SSH remotes

description:
Multiple files contain stale references to JSONL and SQLite that should be updated post-Dolt migration:

1. git.go line ~576: FixMergeDriver sets merge driver name to 'Beads JSONL merge driver'
2. validation.go line ~351: CheckGitConflicts only scans *.jsonl files, ignoring other text files
3. installation.go lines ~93-163: CheckUntrackedBeadsFiles entire check looks for untracked .jsonl files
4. agent.go lines ~718-722: enrichUntrackedFiles references JSONL files
5. gitignore.go lines ~374-387: CheckRedirectTargetValid falls through to checking for beads.db (SQLite)
6. federation.go lines 54,146,263,356,447: return 'N/A (SQLite backend)' messages
7. legacy.go line ~220: CheckDatabaseConfig checks for .db files (SQLite-only)
8. maintenance.go line ~390: message references 'JSONL fallback'
9. role.go line ~31: comment says 'stored in SQLite'
10. legacy.go line ~360: countJSONLIssuesAndPrefix uses default scanner buffer (64KB) unlike migration_validation.go which uses 2MB

Suggested fix: Remove or update references. For checks that only apply to legacy backends, either remove them or clearly gate and document them as legacy-only.
notes: Analysis complete. 10 items to fix across 9 files in cmd/bd/doctor/. Changes: (1) git.go: rename merge driver, (2) validation.go: gate CheckGitConflicts as legacy-only, (3) installation.go: gate CheckUntrackedBeadsFiles as legacy-only, (4) agent.go: update enrichUntrackedFiles message, (5) gitignore.go: add Dolt directory check alongside SQLite .db check, (6) federation.go: change 'SQLite backend' to 'non-Dolt backend', (7) legacy.go: update comment on CheckDatabaseConfig, (8) legacy.go: update CheckFreshClone comment + fix scanner buffer, (9) maintenance.go: update message + document JSONL fallback as legacy, (10) role.go: fix stale SQLite comment.
title: Stale JSONL/SQLite references across doctor subsystem

description: rename.go:23-106: UpdateIssueID does SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 0 then re-enables with SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 1 before tx.Commit(). However, SET statements are session-level in MySQL — they take effect immediately and are NOT rolled back by a transaction rollback. If any operation between disable (line 23) and re-enable (line 103) fails, the defer tx.Rollback() fires but FK checks remain disabled on that pooled connection. Subsequent operations using that connection from the pool will run without FK constraint enforcement. Suggested fix: use a defer to ensure FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS is re-enabled regardless of error path, e.g., defer tx.ExecContext(ctx, 'SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS = 1').
title: SET FOREIGN_KEY_CHECKS=0 not restored on tx rollback

description:
Ensure workspace is pristine before handoff.

**IMPORTANT: Do NOT run `git push`. That is `gt done`'s job (next step).**
**IMPORTANT: Do NOT discard implementation changes. They must already be committed.**

**1. Check for untracked files:**
```bash
git status --porcelain
```
Should be empty. If not:
- Add to .gitignore if appropriate
- Remove if temporary: `rm <file>`
- Commit if needed: `git add <file> && git commit -m "chore: add <file>"`

**2. Check stash:**
```bash
git stash list
```
Should be empty. If not:
- Pop and commit: `git stash pop && git add -A && git commit -m "chore: unstash work (bd-lars)"`
- Or drop if truly garbage: `git stash drop`

**3. Verify clean state and commits:**
```bash
git status                  # Must show "working tree clean"
git stash list              # Must be empty
git log origin/main..HEAD   # Your commits (should show your work)
```

If `git log origin/main..HEAD` shows nothing but you DID make changes,
something went wrong. Do NOT proceed — mail Witness for help.

**Exit criteria:** Workspace clean, no cruft, all work committed. Do NOT push — `gt done` handles that.
title: Clean up workspace

description:
Verify your changes don't break anything and are properly tested.

**1. Run the full test suite:**
```bash
go test ./...               # For Go projects
# Or appropriate command for your stack
```

**ALL TESTS MUST PASS.** Do not proceed with failures.

**2. If tests fail:**
- Read the failure output carefully
- Determine if your change caused it:
  - If yes: Fix it. Return to implement step if needed.
  - If no (pre-existing): File a bead, but still must pass for your PR

```bash
# Check if failure exists on main:
git stash
git checkout main
go test ./...
git checkout -
git stash pop
```

**3. Verify test coverage for new code:**
- New features should have tests
- Bug fixes should have regression tests
- If you added significant code without tests, add them now

**4. Run any other quality checks:**
```bash
# Linting (if configured)
golangci-lint run ./...

# Build check
go build ./...
```

**Exit criteria:** All tests pass, new code has appropriate test coverage.
title: Run tests and verify coverage

description:
Check if the codebase is healthy BEFORE starting your work.

**The Scotty Principle:** Don't walk past a broken warp core. But also don't
let someone else's mess consume your entire mission.

**1. Check tests on main:**
```bash
git stash                   # Save your branch state
git checkout origin/main
go test ./...               # Or appropriate test command
```

**2. If tests PASS:**
```bash
git checkout -              # Back to your branch
git stash pop               # Restore state
```
Continue to implement step.

**3. If tests FAIL on main:**

File a bead and proceed. Do NOT fix pre-existing failures yourself — that is
not your assignment. Your job is to fix the issue on your hook, not main.

| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| Any pre-existing failure | File bead, proceed with your work |

FORBIDDEN: Pushing to main. FORBIDDEN: Fixing pre-existing failures.
You work on YOUR feature branch only. `gt done` handles push/MR.

**File and proceed path:**
```bash
bd create --title "Pre-existing test failure: <description>" --type bug --priority 1

gt mail send <rig>/witness -s "NOTICE: Main has failing tests" -m "Found pre-existing test failures on main.
Filed: <bead-id>
Proceeding with my assigned work (bd-zw81)."

git checkout -
git stash pop
```

**Context consideration:**
If fixing pre-existing failures consumed significant context:
```bash
gt handoff -s "Fixed pre-existing failures, ready for assigned work" -m "Issue: bd-zw81
Fixed: <what you fixed>
Ready to start: implement step"
```
Fresh session continues from implement.

**Exit criteria:** Tests pass on main (or issue filed), ready to implement.
title: Verify tests pass on main

description:
Submit your work and clean up. You cease to exist after this step.

**Self-Cleaning Model:**
Once you run `gt done`, you're gone. The command:
1. Pushes your branch to origin
2. Creates an MR bead in the merge queue
3. Nukes your sandbox (worktree removal)
4. Exits your session immediately

**Run gt done:**
```bash
gt done
```

You should see output like:
```
✓ Work submitted to merge queue
  MR ID: gt-xxxxx
  Source: polecat/<name>
  Target: main
  Issue: bd-8ifp
✓ Sandbox nuked
✓ Session exiting
```

**What happens next (not your concern):**
- Refinery processes your MR from the queue
- Refinery rebases and merges to main
- Refinery closes the issue
- If conflicts: Refinery spawns a FRESH polecat to re-implement

You are NOT involved in any of that. You're gone. Done means gone.

**Exit criteria:** Work submitted, sandbox nuked, session exited.
title: Submit work and self-clean

description:
Ensure ALL implementation work is committed before cleanup.

**CRITICAL: You MUST commit all changes from implementation.**
NEVER use `git checkout -- .` or `git restore .` to discard implementation work.
ALWAYS commit ALL uncommitted changes from your implementation.

**1. Check for uncommitted changes:**
```bash
git status
```

**2. If there are ANY uncommitted changes, commit them now:**
```bash
git add -A && git commit -m "<type>: <descriptive message> (bd-yzlh)"
```

**3. If working tree is already clean, skip.**

**4. VERIFY:**
```bash
git status
```
Must show "nothing to commit, working tree clean".

**5. Verify you have commits:**
```bash
git log origin/main..HEAD --oneline
```
If you made changes during implementation, this MUST show at least 1 commit.

**Exit criteria:** All changes committed. Working tree clean.
title: Commit all implementation changes

description:
Check inbox and handle messages.

```bash
gt mail inbox
```

For each message:

**POLECAT_STARTED**:
A new polecat has started working. Acknowledge and archive.
```bash
# Acknowledge startup (optional: log for activity tracking)
gt mail archive <message-id>
```
No action needed beyond acknowledgment - archive immediately.

**POLECAT_DONE / LIFECYCLE:Shutdown**:

*EPHEMERAL MODEL*: Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission,
recyclable immediately. Once the branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean),
the polecat can be nuked. The MR lifecycle continues independently in the
Refinery. If conflicts arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution
task for a NEW polecat.

Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle: created → queued → processed → merged (handled by Refinery)

The handler (HandlePolecatDone) will:
1. Check cleanup_status from agent bead
2. If "clean" (branch pushed): AUTO-NUKE immediately, archive mail
3. If dirty: Create cleanup wisp for manual intervention

```bash
# The handler does this automatically:
# - For clean state: gt polecat nuke <name> → archive mail
# - For dirty state: create wisp → process in next step
```

Cleanup wisps are only created when something is wrong (uncommitted changes,
unpushed commits). Most POLECAT_DONE messages result in immediate nuke.

**MERGED**:
A branch was merged successfully. This is informational in the ephemeral model
since the polecat was already nuked after MR submission.

If a cleanup wisp exists (dirty state), complete the cleanup:
```bash
# Find the cleanup wisp for this polecat
bd list --wisp --labels=polecat:<name>,state:merge-requested --status=open

# If found, proceed with full polecat nuke:
gt polecat nuke <name>

# Burn the cleanup wisp
bd close <wisp-id>
```
Archive after cleanup is complete.

**HELP / Blocked**:
Assess the request. Can you help? If not, escalate to Mayor:
```bash
gt mail send mayor/ -s "Escalation: <polecat> needs help" -m "<details>"
```
Archive after handling (escalated or resolved):
```bash
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**HANDOFF**:
Read predecessor context. Continue from where they left off.
Archive after absorbing context:
```bash
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**SWARM_START**:
Mayor initiating batch polecat work. Initialize swarm tracking.
```bash
# Parse swarm info from mail body: {"swarm_id": "batch-123", "beads": ["bd-a", "bd-b"]}
bd create --wisp --title "swarm:<swarm_id>" --description "Tracking batch: <swarm_id>" --labels swarm,swarm_id:<swarm_id>,total:<N>,completed:0,start:<timestamp>
```
Archive after creating swarm tracking wisp:
```bash
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**Hygiene principle**: Archive messages after they're fully processed.
Keep only: active work, unprocessed requests. Inbox should be near-empty.
title: Process witness mail

description:
End of patrol cycle decision.

**If context LOW** (can continue patrolling):
1. Generate a brief summary of this patrol cycle
2. Squash the current wisp:
```bash
bd mol squash <mol-id> --summary "<patrol-summary>"
```
3. Create a new patrol wisp:
```bash
bd mol wisp mol-witness-patrol
```
4. Continue executing from the inbox-check step of the new wisp

**If context HIGH** (approaching limit):
1. Write handoff mail with notable observations:
```bash
gt handoff -s "Witness patrol handoff" -m "<observations>"
```
2. Exit cleanly - the daemon will respawn a fresh Witness session

**IMPORTANT**: You must either create a new wisp (context LOW) or exit (context HIGH).
Never leave the session without work on your hook.
title: Loop or exit for respawn

description:
Do the actual implementation work.

**Working principles:**
- Follow existing codebase conventions
- Make atomic, focused commits
- Keep changes scoped to the assigned issue
- Don't gold-plate or scope-creep

**Commit frequently:**
```bash
# After each logical unit of work:
git add <files>
git commit -m "<type>: <description> (bd-lars)"
```

Commit types: feat, fix, refactor, test, docs, chore

**Discovered work:**
If you find bugs or improvements outside your scope:
```bash
bd create --title "Found: <description>" --type bug --priority 2
# Note the ID, continue with your work
```

Do NOT fix unrelated issues in this branch.

**If stuck:**
Don't spin for more than 15 minutes. Mail Witness:
```bash
gt mail send <rig>/witness -s "HELP: Stuck on implementation" -m "Issue: bd-lars
Trying to: <what you're attempting>
Problem: <what's blocking you>
Tried: <what you've attempted>"
```

**Exit criteria:** Implementation complete, all changes committed.
title: Implement the solution

description:
Process cleanup wisps (exception handling for dirty polecats).

In the ephemeral model, cleanup wisps are only created when a polecat has
dirty state (uncommitted changes, unpushed commits) that prevented immediate
nuke. Most polecats are nuked immediately on POLECAT_DONE and never create wisps.

```bash
# Find all cleanup wisps
bd list --wisp --labels=cleanup --status=open
```

If no wisps, skip this step (most common case in ephemeral model).

For each cleanup wisp, investigate and resolve the dirty state:

## State: pending (needs investigation)

1. **Extract polecat name** from wisp title/labels

2. **Diagnose the problem**:
```bash
cd polecats/<name>
git status                    # What's uncommitted?
git stash list                # Any stashed work?
git log origin/main..HEAD     # Any unpushed commits?
```

3. **Resolution options**:
   - **Uncommitted changes**: Commit and push, then nuke
   - **Stashed work**: Pop and commit, or discard if not valuable
   - **Unpushed commits**: Push to origin, then nuke
   - **All valuable work lost**: Escalate to Mayor for recovery

4. **If resolvable locally**: Fix and nuke
```bash
# Example: push unpushed commits
git push origin HEAD

# Then nuke
gt polecat nuke <name>

# Close the wisp
bd close <wisp-id> --reason "Resolved: pushed commits, nuked"
```

5. **If needs escalation**: Send RECOVERY_NEEDED to Mayor
```bash
gt mail send mayor/ -s "RECOVERY_NEEDED <rig>/<polecat>" \
  -m "Cleanup Status: <status>
Branch: <branch>
Issue: <issue-id>

Cannot auto-resolve. Please advise."
```
Leave wisp open until Mayor resolves.

## State: merge-requested (legacy, rare)

This state was used before the ephemeral model. If found, the polecat is
waiting for a MERGED signal. The inbox-check step handles these.

**Parallelism**: Use Task tool subagents to process multiple cleanups concurrently.
Each cleanup is independent - perfect for parallel execution.
title: Process pending cleanup wisps

description:
Review your own changes before running tests.

**1. Review the diff:**
```bash
git diff origin/main...HEAD     # All changes vs main
git log --oneline origin/main..HEAD  # All commits
```

**2. Check for common issues:**

| Category | Look For |
|----------|----------|
| Bugs | Off-by-one, null handling, edge cases |
| Security | Injection, auth bypass, exposed secrets |
| Style | Naming, formatting, code organization |
| Completeness | Missing error handling, incomplete paths |
| Cruft | Debug prints, commented code, TODOs |

**3. Fix issues found:**
Don't just note them - fix them now. Amend or add commits as needed.

**4. Verify no unintended changes:**
```bash
git diff --stat origin/main...HEAD
# Only files relevant to bd-7fsv should appear
```

If you accidentally modified unrelated files, remove those changes.

**Exit criteria:** Changes are clean, reviewed, and ready for testing.

description:
Check for expired timer gates and escalate as needed.

Timer gates are async wait conditions with a timeout. When the timeout expires,
the gate should be escalated to the overseer for human intervention.

**Step 1: Run timer gate check**
```bash
bd gate check --type=timer --escalate
```

This command:
1. Finds all open gate issues with await_type=timer
2. Checks if `now > created_at + timeout`
3. Escalates expired gates via `gt escalate` (HIGH severity)
4. Reports summary of gate status

**Step 2: Review output**

If expired gates were found and escalated:
- The escalation creates an audit trail bead
- Overseer will be notified via mail
- Gate remains open until manually resolved

If no expired gates:
- Continue patrol normally

**Note**: Timer gates do NOT auto-close on expiration. They escalate.
This ensures human oversight of timeout conditions.

**Parallelism**: This is a single command, no parallel execution needed.
title: Check timer gates for expiration

description:
Ensure workspace is pristine before handoff.

**IMPORTANT: Do NOT run `git push`. That is `gt done`'s job (next step).**
**IMPORTANT: Do NOT discard implementation changes. They must already be committed.**

**1. Check for untracked files:**
```bash
git status --porcelain
```
Should be empty. If not:
- Add to .gitignore if appropriate
- Remove if temporary: `rm <file>`
- Commit if needed: `git add <file> && git commit -m "chore: add <file>"`

**2. Check stash:**
```bash
git stash list
```
Should be empty. If not:
- Pop and commit: `git stash pop && git add -A && git commit -m "chore: unstash work (bd-agx1)"`
- Or drop if truly garbage: `git stash drop`

**3. Verify clean state and commits:**
```bash
git status                  # Must show "working tree clean"
git stash list              # Must be empty
git log origin/main..HEAD   # Your commits (should show your work)
```

If `git log origin/main..HEAD` shows nothing but you DID make changes,
something went wrong. Do NOT proceed — mail Witness for help.

**Exit criteria:** Workspace clean, no cruft, all work committed. Do NOT push — `gt done` handles that.

description:
Ensure ALL implementation work is committed before cleanup.

**CRITICAL: You MUST commit all changes from implementation.**
NEVER use `git checkout -- .` or `git restore .` to discard implementation work.
ALWAYS commit ALL uncommitted changes from your implementation.

**1. Check for uncommitted changes:**
```bash
git status
```

**2. If there are ANY uncommitted changes, commit them now:**
```bash
git add -A && git commit -m "<type>: <descriptive message> (bd-w59l)"
```

**3. If working tree is already clean, skip.**

**4. VERIFY:**
```bash
git status
```
Must show "nothing to commit, working tree clean".

**5. Verify you have commits:**
```bash
git log origin/main..HEAD --oneline
```
If you made changes during implementation, this MUST show at least 1 commit.

**Exit criteria:** All changes committed. Working tree clean.

**1. Check current branch state:**

```

# Standard naming: polecat/<your-name> or feature/<issue-id>
git checkout -b polecat/<name>
```

**3. Ensure clean working state:**
```bash

git stash list              # Should be empty
```

If dirty state from previous work:
```bash

# If changes are unrelated cruft:
git stash push -m "unrelated changes before bd-9k0c"
# Or discard if truly garbage:

```

**4. Sync with main:**
```bash
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main      # Get latest, rebase your branch
```

If rebase conflicts:
- Resolve them carefully
- Test after resolution
- If stuck, mail Witness

**Exit criteria:** You're on a clean feature branch, rebased on latest main.

description:
Process cleanup wisps (exception handling for dirty polecats).

In the ephemeral model, cleanup wisps are only created when a polecat has
dirty state (uncommitted changes, unpushed commits) that prevented immediate
nuke. Most polecats are nuked immediately on POLECAT_DONE and never create wisps.

```bash
# Find all cleanup wisps
bd list --wisp --labels=cleanup --status=open
```

If no wisps, skip this step (most common case in ephemeral model).

For each cleanup wisp, investigate and resolve the dirty state:

## State: pending (needs investigation)

1. **Extract polecat name** from wisp title/labels

2. **Diagnose the problem**:
```bash
cd polecats/<name>
git status                    # What's uncommitted?
git stash list                # Any stashed work?
git log origin/main..HEAD     # Any unpushed commits?
```

3. **Resolution options**:
   - **Uncommitted changes**: Commit and push, then nuke
   - **Stashed work**: Pop and commit, or discard if not valuable
   - **Unpushed commits**: Push to origin, then nuke
   - **All valuable work lost**: Escalate to Mayor for recovery

4. **If resolvable locally**: Fix and nuke
```bash
# Example: push unpushed commits
git push origin HEAD

# Then nuke
gt polecat nuke <name>

# Close the wisp
bd close <wisp-id> --reason "Resolved: pushed commits, nuked"
```

5. **If needs escalation**: Send RECOVERY_NEEDED to Mayor
```bash
gt mail send mayor/ -s "RECOVERY_NEEDED <rig>/<polecat>" \
  -m "Cleanup Status: <status>
Branch: <branch>
Issue: <issue-id>

Cannot auto-resolve. Please advise."
```
Leave wisp open until Mayor resolves.

## State: merge-requested (legacy, rare)

This state was used before the ephemeral model. If found, the polecat is
waiting for a MERGED signal. The inbox-check step handles these.

**Parallelism**: Use Task tool subagents to process multiple cleanups concurrently.
Each cleanup is independent - perfect for parallel execution.
title: Process pending cleanup wisps

description:
Survey all polecats using agent beads (ZFC: trust what agents report).

**Step 1: List polecat agent beads**

```bash
bd list --type=agent --json
```

Filter the JSON output for entries where description contains `role_type: polecat`.
Each polecat agent bead has fields in its description:
- `role_type: polecat`
- `rig: <rig-name>`
- `agent_state: running|orphaned|stuck|done`
- `hook_bead: <current-work-id>`

**Step 2: For each polecat, check agent_state**

| agent_state | Meaning | Action |
|-------------|---------|--------|
| running | Actively working | Check progress (Step 3) |
| orphaned | Crashed before self-nuke | Auto-nuke if clean (Step 3a) |
| stuck | Self-reported stuck | Handle stuck protocol |
| done | Work complete | Verify cleanup triggered (see Step 4a) |

**Step 3: For running polecats, assess progress**

Check the hook_bead field to see what they're working on:
```bash
bd show <hook_bead>  # See current step/issue
```

You can also verify they're responsive:
```bash
tmux capture-pane -t gt-<rig>-<name> -p | tail -20
```

Look for:
- Recent tool activity → making progress
- Inactive at prompt → may need nudge
- Error messages → may need help

**Step 3a: For orphaned polecats, auto-nuke if clean**

When a polecat has no hook_bead, it likely crashed before running gt done (orphaned). Check if it's safe to nuke:

```bash
# Check git status in the polecat's worktree
cd polecats/<name>
git status --porcelain         # Should be empty (clean)
git log origin/main..HEAD      # Should have no unpushed commits
```

**If clean** (no uncommitted changes, no unpushed commits):
```bash
# Safe to nuke - no work to lose
gt polecat nuke <name>
```
Log the auto-nuke for audit purposes. No escalation needed.

**If dirty** (uncommitted or unpushed work):
```bash
# Escalate to Mayor - polecat has work that might be valuable
gt mail send mayor/ -s \"ORPHAN_DIRTY: <polecat> has uncommitted work\" \
  -m \"Polecat: <name>
State: orphaned (crashed before self-nuke)
Git status: <uncommitted-files>
Unpushed commits: <count>

Please advise: recover work or discard?\"
```

**Rationale**: Orphaned polecats with clean git state are pure overhead. They have
no work and no state worth preserving. Nuking them immediately frees resources
and reduces noise. Only escalate when there's actual work at risk.

**Step 4: Decide action**

| Observation | Action |
|-------------|--------|
| agent_state=running, recent activity | None |
| agent_state=running, inactive 5-15 min | Gentle nudge |
| agent_state=running, inactive 15+ min | Direct nudge with deadline |
| agent_state=stuck | Assess and help or escalate |
| agent_state=done | Verify cleanup triggered (see Step 4a) |

**Step 4a: Handle agent_state=done**

In the ephemeral model, polecats with agent_state=done and cleanup_status=clean
should already be nuked by HandlePolecatDone. Finding one here indicates:

1. **Stale agent bead** - polecat was nuked but bead remains
   ```bash
   # Verify polecat doesn't exist anymore
   ls polecats/<name> 2>/dev/null || echo "Already nuked"
   ```
   If nuked, the agent bead is stale. Clean it up or ignore.

2. **Cleanup wisp exists** - polecat has dirty state needing intervention
   ```bash
   bd list --wisp --labels=polecat:<name> --status=open
   ```
   Process in process-cleanups step.

3. **No wisp, polecat exists** - POLECAT_DONE mail was missed
   Try auto-nuke directly (ephemeral model):
   ```bash
   # Check cleanup_status and nuke if clean
   gt polecat nuke <name>  # Will fail if dirty
   ```
   If nuke fails (dirty state), create cleanup wisp for investigation.

**Step 5: Execute nudges**
```bash
gt nudge <rig>/polecats/<name> "How's progress? Need help?"
```

**Step 6: Escalate if needed**
```bash
gt mail send mayor/ -s "Escalation: <polecat> stuck" \
  -m "Polecat <name> reports stuck. Please intervene."
```

**Parallelism**: Use Task tool subagents to inspect multiple polecats concurrently.

**ZFC Principle**: Trust agent_state from beads. Don't infer state from PID/tmux.
title: Inspect all active polecats

description:
Pick next branch from queue. Attempt mechanical rebase on current main.

**Step 1: Checkout and attempt rebase**
```bash
git checkout -b temp origin/<polecat-branch>
git rebase origin/main
```

**Step 2: Check rebase result**

The rebase exits with:
- Exit code 0: Success - proceed to run-tests
- Exit code 1 (conflicts): Conflict detected - proceed to Step 3

To detect conflict state after rebase fails:
```bash
# Check if we're in a conflicted rebase state
ls .git/rebase-merge 2>/dev/null && echo "CONFLICT_STATE"
```

**Step 3: Handle conflicts (if any)**

If rebase SUCCEEDED (exit code 0):
- Skip to run-tests step (continue normal merge flow)

If rebase FAILED with conflicts:

1. **Abort the rebase** (DO NOT leave repo in conflicted state):
```bash
git rebase --abort
```

2. **Record conflict metadata**:
```bash
# Capture main SHA for reference
MAIN_SHA=$(git rev-parse origin/main)
BRANCH_SHA=$(git rev-parse origin/<polecat-branch>)
```

3. **Create conflict-resolution task**:
```bash
bd create --type=task --priority=1 --title="Resolve merge conflicts: <original-issue-title>" --description="## Conflict Resolution Required

Original MR: <mr-bead-id>
Branch: <polecat-branch>
Original Issue: <issue-id>
Conflict with main at: ${MAIN_SHA}
Branch SHA: ${BRANCH_SHA}

## Instructions
1. Clone/checkout the branch
2. Rebase on current main: git rebase origin/main
3. Resolve conflicts
4. Force push: git push -f origin <branch>
5. Close this task when done

The MR will be re-queued for processing after conflicts are resolved."
```

4. **Skip this MR** (do NOT delete branch or close MR bead):
- Leave branch intact for conflict resolution
- Leave MR bead open (will be re-processed after resolution)
- Continue to loop-check for next branch

**CRITICAL**: Never delete a branch that has conflicts. The branch contains
the original work and must be preserved for conflict resolution.

Track: rebase result (success/conflict), conflict task ID if created.
title: Mechanical rebase

description:
Initialize your session and understand your assignment.

**1. Prime your environment:**
```bash
gt prime                    # Load role context
bd prime                    # Load beads context
```

**2. Check your hook:**
```bash
gt hook               # Shows your pinned molecule and hook_bead
```

The hook_bead is your assigned issue. Read it carefully:
```bash
bd show bd-7zej           # Full issue details
```

**3. Check inbox for additional context:**
```bash
gt mail inbox
# Read any HANDOFF or assignment messages
```

**4. Understand the requirements:**
- What exactly needs to be done?
- What files are likely involved?
- Are there dependencies or blockers?
- What does "done" look like?

**5. Verify you can proceed:**
- No unresolved blockers on the issue
- You understand what to do
- Required resources are available

If blocked or unclear, mail Witness immediately:
```bash
gt mail send <rig>/witness -s "HELP: Unclear requirements" -m "Issue: bd-7zej
Question: <what you need clarified>"
```

**Exit criteria:** You understand the work and can begin implementation.
title: Load context and verify assignment

description:
Check if the codebase is healthy BEFORE starting your work.

**The Scotty Principle:** Don't walk past a broken warp core. But also don't
let someone else's mess consume your entire mission.

**1. Check tests on main:**
```bash
git stash                   # Save your branch state
git checkout origin/main
go test ./...               # Or appropriate test command
```

**2. If tests PASS:**
```bash
git checkout -              # Back to your branch
git stash pop               # Restore state
```
Continue to implement step.

**3. If tests FAIL on main:**

File a bead and proceed. Do NOT fix pre-existing failures yourself — that is
not your assignment. Your job is to fix the issue on your hook, not main.

| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| Any pre-existing failure | File bead, proceed with your work |

FORBIDDEN: Pushing to main. FORBIDDEN: Fixing pre-existing failures.
You work on YOUR feature branch only. `gt done` handles push/MR.

**File and proceed path:**
```bash
bd create --title "Pre-existing test failure: <description>" --type bug --priority 1

gt mail send <rig>/witness -s "NOTICE: Main has failing tests" -m "Found pre-existing test failures on main.
Filed: <bead-id>
Proceeding with my assigned work (bd-yzlh)."

git checkout -
git stash pop
```

**Context consideration:**
If fixing pre-existing failures consumed significant context:
```bash
gt handoff -s "Fixed pre-existing failures, ready for assigned work" -m "Issue: bd-yzlh
Fixed: <what you fixed>
Ready to start: implement step"
```
Fresh session continues from implement.

**Exit criteria:** Tests pass on main (or issue filed), ready to implement.
title: Verify tests pass on main

description:
Send WITNESS_PING to Deacon for second-order monitoring.

The Witness fleet collectively monitors Deacon health - this prevents the
"who watches the watchers" problem. If Deacon dies, Witnesses detect it.

**Step 1: Send ping**
```bash
gt mail send deacon/ -s "WITNESS_PING <rig>" -m "Rig: <rig>
Timestamp: $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)
Patrol: <cycle-number>"
```

**Step 2: Check Deacon health**
```bash
# Check Deacon agent bead for last_activity
bd list --type=agent --json | jq '.[] | select(.description | contains("deacon"))'
```

Look at the `last_activity` timestamp. If stale (>5 minutes since last update):
- Deacon may be dead or stuck

**Step 3: Escalate if needed**
```bash
# If Deacon appears down
gt mail send mayor/ -s "ALERT: Deacon appears unresponsive" -m "No Deacon activity for >5 minutes.
Last seen: <timestamp>
Witness: <rig>/witness"
```

Note: Multiple Witnesses may send this alert. Mayor should handle deduplication.
title: Ping Deacon for health check

description:
Ensure you're on a clean feature branch ready for work.

**1. Check current branch state:**
```bash
git status
git branch --show-current
```

**2. If not on a feature branch, create one:**
```bash
# Standard naming: polecat/<your-name> or feature/<issue-id>
git checkout -b polecat/<name>
```

**3. Ensure clean working state:**
```bash
git status                  # Should show "working tree clean"
git stash list              # Should be empty
```

If dirty state from previous work:
```bash
# If changes are relevant to this issue:
git add -A && git commit -m "WIP: <description>"

# If changes are unrelated cruft:
git stash push -m "unrelated changes before bd-zw81"
# Or discard if truly garbage:
git checkout -- .
```

**4. Sync with main:**
```bash
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main      # Get latest, rebase your branch
```

If rebase conflicts:
- Resolve them carefully
- Test after resolution
- If stuck, mail Witness

**Exit criteria:** You're on a clean feature branch, rebased on latest main.
title: Set up working branch
